topics.title: Heat Transfer by Natural Convection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL408 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Heat Transfer by Natural Convection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Heat Transfer by Natural Convection — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Natural Convection Heat Transfer** is defined as the mode of heat transfer that occurs between a solid surface and a fluid (liquid or gas) in contact with it, where the fluid motion is generated **solely by buoyancy forces** arising from the **density difference** caused by temperature gradients within the fluid — without the aid of any external agency like a pump, blower, or fan.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech lab curriculum (PCCHL408 — Heat Transfer Operations Lab, Module Experiment 2), the **objective** is to experimentally determine the **natural convection heat transfer coefficient ($h_{c}$)** from a heated vertical or horizontal cylinder/tube to the surrounding ambient air, and thereby compute the **Nusselt Number (Nu)** as a function of the **Grashof Number (Gr)** and **Prandtl Number (Pr)**.

Mathematically, the convective heat transfer rate is governed by **Newton's Law of Cooling**:

$$q = h_{c} \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$

Where:
- $q$ = Heat transfer rate (**W**)
- $h_{c}$ = Convective heat transfer coefficient (**W/m²·K**)
- $A$ = Heat transfer area (**m²**)
- $\Delta T = (T_{s} - T_{\infty})$ = Temperature difference between surface and ambient (**K** or **°C**)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The Boiling Pot Analogy** 🍲
> Imagine a kettle of water placed on a stove (without a fan blowing across it). The water at the bottom heats up, becomes lighter (less dense), and rises. Cooler, denser water descends to take its place. This **self-sustaining circulation** is natural convection. The kettle didn't have a pump — the heat itself drove the motion through buoyancy. In our lab experiment, a heated **test cylinder** plays the role of the stove, and **surrounding air** plays the role of the water.

A more **engineering-flavored** analogy:
> Place a hot iron rod vertically in still air. The air touching the rod heats up, expands, becomes buoyant, and rises in a thin **boundary layer** along the rod's surface. Cooler ambient air rushes in from below to replace it. This forms a continuous **laminar plume** — the fundamental physical phenomenon our experiment aims to quantify.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Properties of Air at Film Temperature** (used universally in lab calculations):
> - Thermal conductivity of air, $k \approx \mathbf{0.025}$–$\mathbf{0.030}$ **W/m·K**
> - Kinematic viscosity of air, $\nu \approx \mathbf{1.5 \times 10^{-5}}$–$\mathbf{1.8 \times 10^{-5}}$ **m²/s**
> - Prandtl Number of air, $Pr \approx \mathbf{0.71}$ (dimensionless)
> - Volumetric expansion coefficient, $\beta = \mathbf{1/T_{film}}$ for ideal gases (**1/K**)
> - Acceleration due to gravity, $g = \mathbf{9.81}$ **m/s²**

> [!NOTE]
> The **Film Temperature** is calculated as the arithmetic mean of the surface temperature ($T_{s}$) and ambient temperature ($T_{\infty}$):
> $T_{film} = \dfrac{T_{s} + T_{\infty}}{2}$, expressed in **Kelvin (K)**.

---

## 1.4 Governing Dimensionless Numbers — First Glimpse

The three **dimensionless numbers** that govern natural convection are:

| Number | Symbol | Physical Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Nusselt Number** | $Nu$ | Ratio of convective to conductive heat transfer across the fluid boundary layer |
| **Grashof Number** | $Gr$ | Ratio of buoyancy to viscous forces in the fluid |
| **Prandtl Number** | $Pr$ | Ratio of momentum diffusivity to thermal diffusivity |

These are the **pillars** of the entire analysis that follows.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Mechanism — Step by Step

Natural convection arises through the following structured physical sequence:

1. **Thermal Boundary Layer Formation**:
   When a hot surface ($T_{s}$) is exposed to quiescent ambient fluid ($T_{\infty}$), a thin layer of fluid near the surface — called the **thermal boundary layer (δₜ)** — experiences a temperature gradient. The thickness of this layer grows as one moves along the surface.

2. **Density Variation**:
   The heated fluid within this boundary layer experiences a drop in density according to:
   $$\rho_{heated} = \dfrac{\rho_{\infty}}{(1 + \beta \cdot \Delta T)}$$

3. **Buoyancy Force Activation**:
   The density difference creates a **buoyancy force**:
   $$F_{buoyancy} = (\rho_{\infty} - \rho_{heated}) \cdot g \cdot \forall$$

4. **Fluid Motion (Plume Formation)**:
   The buoyant fluid rises (for a hot surface facing up or vertical) or slides (for a hot surface facing down), dragging adjacent fluid through **viscous coupling**, creating a **velocity boundary layer (δᵥ)**.

5. **Heat Transfer to Ambient**:
   Energy is transported from the surface through this moving fluid to the surroundings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction (KTU Board Favourite Question)**:
> In **forced convection**, motion is imposed externally and the **Reynolds Number (Re)** governs flow. In **natural convection**, motion is internally driven and the **Grashof Number (Gr)** replaces Re.

---

## 2.2 Derivation of the Dimensionless Numbers

### 2.2.1 Nusselt Number (Nu)

$$Nu = \dfrac{h_{c} \cdot L}{k_{f}}$$

Where $L$ is the **characteristic length** (for a vertical cylinder, $L$ = its length/height; for a horizontal cylinder, $L$ = its outer diameter $D$).

### 2.2.2 Grashof Number (Gr)

$$Gr = \dfrac{g \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T \cdot L^{3}}{\nu^{2}}$$

Where:
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $\beta$ = volumetric thermal expansion coefficient (**1/K**)
- $\Delta T$ = $(T_{s} - T_{\infty})$
- $L$ = characteristic length
- $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity of the fluid (**m²/s**)

### 2.2.3 Prandtl Number (Pr)

$$Pr = \dfrac{\mu \cdot c_{p}}{k_{f}} = \dfrac{\nu}{\alpha}$$

Where $\mu$ is dynamic viscosity, $c_{p}$ is specific heat, $k_{f}$ is thermal conductivity, and $\alpha$ is thermal diffusivity.

### 2.2.4 Rayleigh Number (Ra)

$$Ra = Gr \cdot Pr = \dfrac{g \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T \cdot L^{3}}{\nu \cdot \alpha}$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **complete, exam-ready** summary of every equation a student needs to compute $h_{c}$ and validate experimental results in the KTU lab viva and external exam.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat input rate | $Q = V \cdot I$ | **W** |
| 2 | Heat lost to convection | $Q_{conv} = Q - Q_{rad} - Q_{loss}$ | **W** |
| 3 | Newton's Law of Cooling | $q = h_{c} \cdot A \cdot (T_{s} - T_{\infty})$ | **W** |
| 4 | Convective coefficient | $h_{c} = \dfrac{Q_{conv}}{A \cdot (T_{s} - T_{\infty})}$ | **W/m²·K** |
| 5 | Nusselt Number | $Nu = \dfrac{h_{c} \cdot L}{k_{f}}$ | — |
| 6 | Grashof Number | $Gr = \dfrac{g \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T \cdot L^{3}}{\nu^{2}}$ | — |
| 7 | Prandtl Number | $Pr = \dfrac{c_{p} \cdot \mu}{k_{f}}$ | — |
| 8 | Rayleigh Number | $Ra = Gr \cdot Pr$ | — |
| 9 | Film Temperature | $T_{f} = \dfrac{T_{s} + T_{\infty}}{2}$ | **K** |
| 10 | Volumetric expansion (gas) | $\beta = \dfrac{1}{T_{f}}$ | **1/K** |
| 11 | Heat transfer area (vertical cyl.) | $A = \pi \cdot D \cdot L$ | **m²** |
| 12 | Radiation loss (Stefan's Law) | $Q_{rad} = \sigma \cdot A \cdot (T_{s}^{4} - T_{\infty}^{4})$ | **W** |

---

## 2.4 KTU-Empirical Correlations for Natural Convection

The **Nusselt Number** is correlated to **Gr** and **Pr** through experimentally validated empirical equations. The form depends on the **regime** (laminar, transition, turbulent):

### For a **Vertical Cylinder/Plate**:

| Regime | Rayleigh Range | Correlation |
|---|---|---|
| Laminar | $Ra_{L} \leq 10^{9}$ | $Nu = 0.59 \cdot Ra^{1/4}$ |
| Turbulent | $Ra_{L} \geq 10^{9}$ | $Nu = 0.10 \cdot Ra^{1/3}$ |

### For a **Horizontal Cylinder**:

$$Nu = C \cdot (Gr \cdot Pr)^{n} = C \cdot Ra^{n}$$

| Regime | $Ra_{D}$ Range | C | n |
|---|---|---|---|
| Laminar | $10^{4}$ to $10^{7}$ | **0.53** | 0.25 |
| Transition | $10^{7}$ to $10^{9}$ | **0.13** | 0.33 |
| Turbulent | $10^{9}$ to $10^{12}$ | **0.10** | 0.33 |

> [!WARNING]
> The **KTU board examiner** specifically checks whether the student has **identified the correct regime** (laminar/transition/turbulent) before plugging into the correlation. Marks are deducted for blindly using any single formula.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Heat exchanger design** (shell-and-tube, finned tubes)
- **Cooling of nuclear reactor cores** (in case of pump failure — passive safety)
- **Building HVAC and ventilation design**
- **Solar thermal collectors** (passive solar architecture)
- **Cooling of high-power electronics** and **transformers**
- **Geophysical flows** — atmospheric wind, ocean currents

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Grashof Number from First Principles

The Grashof Number represents the **ratio of buoyancy force to viscous force** acting on a fluid element.

**Step 1 — Buoyancy Force on a fluid parcel of characteristic size $L$**:
$$F_{b} \sim \Delta \rho \cdot g \cdot L^{3}$$

**Step 2 — Density difference in terms of temperature**:
Using the **Boussinesq approximation** ($\beta \cdot \Delta T \ll 1$):
$$\Delta \rho = \rho_{\infty} \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 3 — Substituting**:
$$F_{b} \sim \rho_{\infty} \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T \cdot g \cdot L^{3}$$

**Step 4 — Viscous Force** (from Newton's law of viscosity):
$$F_{v} \sim \mu \cdot \dfrac{V}{L} \cdot L^{2} = \mu \cdot V \cdot L$$

**Step 5 — Characteristic velocity from buoyancy-viscous balance**:
Setting $F_{b} \sim F_{v}$:
$$\rho \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T \cdot g \cdot L^{3} \sim \mu \cdot V \cdot L$$
$$V \sim \dfrac{\rho \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T \cdot g \cdot L^{2}}{\mu}$$

**Step 6 — Defining Grashof Number**:
$$Gr = \dfrac{F_{b}}{F_{v}} = \dfrac{\rho^{2} \cdot g \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T \cdot L^{3}}{\mu^{2}}$$

Using $\nu = \mu / \rho$:
$$Gr = \dfrac{g \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T \cdot L^{3}}{\nu^{2}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Convective Heat Transfer Coefficient from Energy Balance

**Step 1 — Total heat input** to the heater coil (measured experimentally):
$$Q_{input} = V \cdot I$$

**Step 2 — Heat loss by radiation** from the test cylinder:
$$Q_{rad} = \sigma \cdot A \cdot (T_{s}^{4} - T_{\infty}^{4})$$
Where $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ **W/m²·K⁴** is the **Stefan-Boltzmann constant**.

**Step 3 — Conduction and other losses** are estimated or neglected:
$$Q_{loss} \approx 0 \text{ to } 10\% \text{ of } Q_{input}$$

**Step 4 — Net heat transferred by natural convection**:
$$Q_{conv} = Q_{input} - Q_{rad} - Q_{loss}$$

**Step 5 — From Newton's Law of Cooling**:
$$h_{c} = \dfrac{Q_{conv}}{A \cdot (T_{s} - T_{\infty})}$$

**Step 6 — Nusselt Number**:
$$Nu = \dfrac{h_{c} \cdot D}{k_{f}}$$

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Numerical Sample — KTU Style

> [!NOTE]
> The following worked example is the **typical numerical** asked in KTU 2024 Scheme lab exams. Work through it line-by-line to master the topic.

### Given Data
- Test cylinder diameter, $D = 0.04$ **m**
- Test cylinder length, $L = 0.5$ **m**
- Heater voltage, $V = 40$ **V**
- Heater current, $I = 2$ **A**
- Surface temperature, $T_{s} = 70$ **°C** = **343 K**
- Ambient temperature, $T_{\infty} = 30$ **°C** = **303 K**
- Thermal conductivity of air at film temp, $k_{f} = 0.0275$ **W/m·K**
- Kinematic viscosity of air at film temp, $\nu = 1.65 \times 10^{-5}$ **m²/s**
- Prandtl Number of air, $Pr = 0.71$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Total heat input**:
$$Q_{input} = V \cdot I = 40 \times 2 = 80 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Heat transfer area**:
$$A = \pi \cdot D \cdot L = \pi \times 0.04 \times 0.5 = 0.0628 \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Film Temperature**:
$$T_{f} = \dfrac{T_{s} + T_{\infty}}{2} = \dfrac{343 + 303}{2} = 323 \text{ K}$$

**Step 4 — Volumetric expansion coefficient**:
$$\beta = \dfrac{1}{T_{f}} = \dfrac{1}{323} = 3.096 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Temperature difference**:
$$\Delta T = T_{s} - T_{\infty} = 70 - 30 = 40 \text{ K}$$

**Step 6 — Grashof Number**:
$$Gr = \dfrac{g \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T \cdot D^{3}}{\nu^{2}}$$

Substituting:
$$Gr = \dfrac{9.81 \times (3.096 \times 10^{-3}) \times 40 \times (0.04)^{3}}{(1.65 \times 10^{-5})^{2}}$$

**Step 7 — Numerator evaluation**:
$$9.81 \times 3.096 \times 10^{-3} = 3.037 \times 10^{-2}$$
$$3.037 \times 10^{-2} \times 40 = 1.215$$
$$1.215 \times (0.04)^{3} = 1.215 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-5} = 7.776 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 8 — Denominator evaluation**:
$$(1.65 \times 10^{-5})^{2} = 2.7225 \times 10^{-10}$$

**Step 9 — Final Grashof Number**:
$$Gr = \dfrac{7.776 \times 10^{-5}}{2.7225 \times 10^{-10}} = 2.857 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step 10 — Rayleigh Number**:
$$Ra = Gr \cdot Pr = 2.857 \times 10^{5} \times 0.71 = 2.028 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step 11 — Regime Identification**:
$$Ra = 2.028 \times 10^{5} \in [10^{4}, 10^{7}] \Rightarrow \textbf{Laminar regime}$$

**Step 12 — Apply horizontal cylinder correlation**:
$$Nu = 0.53 \cdot Ra^{1/4}$$
$$Ra^{1/4} = (2.028 \times 10^{5})^{0.25}$$
$$\log_{10}(Ra) = 5.307$$
$$0.25 \times 5.307 = 1.327$$
$$Ra^{1/4} = 10^{1.327} = 21.23$$

**Step 13 — Nusselt Number**:
$$Nu = 0.53 \times 21.23 = 11.25$$

**Step 14 — Convective Heat Transfer Coefficient**:
$$h_{c} = \dfrac{Nu \cdot k_{f}}{D} = \dfrac{11.25 \times 0.0275}{0.04} = 7.73 \text{ W/m}^{2}\text{·K}$$

**Step 15 — Verification via Newton's Law**:
$$Q_{conv, \text{calc}} = h_{c} \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 7.73 \times 0.0628 \times 40 = 19.43 \text{ W}$$

Theoretical (from correlation) and experimental values can now be compared. The **percentage error** is the standard KTU validation metric.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Heat Transfer by Natural Convection Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Power Supply 0-230V] --> B[Variac Autotransformer]
    B --> C[Heater Coil inside Test Cylinder]
    C --> D[Heated Test Cylinder D and L]
    D --> E[Surface Temperature Ts via Thermocouple]
    D --> F[Ambient Air Temperature Tinf via Thermocouple]
    D --> G[Surrounding Quiescent Air Boundary Layer]
    G --> H[Buoyancy Driven Plume Rise]
    H --> I[Heat Dissipated to Ambient]
    C --> J[Voltmeter V and Ammeter I]
    J --> K[Heat Input Q = V x I]
    K --> L[Qrad via Stefan Boltzmann Law]
    L --> M[Qconv = Q - Qrad - Qloss]
    M --> N[hc = Qconv over A into Delta T]
    E --> O[Nu = hc x L over kf]
    F --> P[Gr = g x beta x dT x L cubed over nu squared]
    P --> Q[Ra = Gr x Pr]
    Q --> R[Nu correlation from empirical chart]
    R --> S{Nu predicted vs Nu experimental}
    S --> T[Compute Percentage Error]
    S --> U[Validate Natural Convection Theory]
```

---


---

## 4.3 Boundary Layer Schematic — Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Hot Surface at Ts] --> L2[Thermal Boundary Layer deltat]
    L2 --> L3[Velocity Boundary Layer deltav]
    L3 --> L4[Undisturbed Ambient Fluid at Tinf]
    L1 -- Conduction through thin fluid layer --> L2
    L2 -- Convection via buoyant plume --> L3
    L3 -- No motion in quiescent zone --> L4
    style L1 fill:#ff6b6b
    style L2 fill:#ffa500
    style L3 fill:#ffd700
    style L4 fill:#87ceeb
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**:
> - **Red zone (L1)**: Solid surface at temperature $T_{s}$ — energy source.
> - **Orange zone (L2)**: Thermal boundary layer — temperature drops from $T_{s}$ to $T_{\infty}$.
> - **Yellow zone (L3)**: Velocity boundary layer — fluid moves upward due to buoyancy.
> - **Blue zone (L4)**: Quiescent ambient air — no velocity, no temperature gradient.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Nusselt Number. What is its physical significance in natural convection heat transfer?**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

The **Nusselt Number** is a dimensionless number defined as the ratio of convective to conductive heat transfer across the fluid boundary layer adjacent to a surface.

$$Nu = \dfrac{h_{c} \cdot L}{k_{f}}$$

- It is a measure of the **enhancement of heat transfer** through a fluid due to convection compared to pure conduction.
- When $Nu = 1$, heat transfer is purely by conduction.
- When $Nu \gg 1$, convection dominates.
- In natural convection, $Nu$ is functionally related to $Gr$ and $Pr$ through empirical correlations of the form $Nu = C (Gr \cdot Pr)^{n}$.

*(Examiner allocates: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for formula + 1 mark for physical significance)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between forced and natural convection. State the governing dimensionless parameters in each case.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

| Feature | Forced Convection | Natural Convection |
|---|---|---|
| Driving force | External agency (pump, fan) | Buoyancy from density difference |
| Governing number | Reynolds Number (Re) | Grashof Number (Gr) |
| Fluid motion | Imposed, controllable | Self-induced, depends on $\Delta T$ |
| Velocity scale | Externally fixed | Function of $\Delta T, L, g, \beta, \nu$ |

In natural convection, the combined parameter **Rayleigh Number (Ra = Gr·Pr)** dictates the heat transfer rate.

*(Examiner allocates: 1 mark for driving force + 1 mark for governing number + 1 mark for velocity distinction)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the experimental setup to determine the natural convection heat transfer coefficient from a horizontal cylinder. List the instruments and measurements required.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A horizontal cylinder of diameter 50 mm and length 0.6 m is heated electrically. The surface temperature is 80 °C and the ambient air temperature is 30 °C. The heater consumes 120 W. Neglecting heat losses by radiation, calculate the heat transfer coefficient and the Nusselt Number. Take $k_{f} = 0.028$ W/m·K. Comment on the regime of flow.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — [7 Marks]**

**Apparatus**:
- A horizontal **brass test cylinder** with embedded nichrome heater coil
- **Variac** (auto-transformer) — 0 to 230 V
- **Voltmeter** and **Ammeter** — to measure power input
- **Two calibrated thermocouples** (Chromel-Alumel / K-type) — one for surface, one for ambient
- **Digital Temperature Indicator (DTI)** with cold junction compensation
- Insulated end caps to minimize axial losses

**Procedure (Stepwise)**:

1. **Switch ON** the apparatus. Allow a **steady state** period of 15-20 minutes.
2. Set the variac to a low voltage. Note $V$, $I$, $T_{s}$, $T_{\infty}$.
3. Increment the voltage in 4-5 steps to obtain a range of $\Delta T$ values.
4. At each step, **wait for steady state** (DTI readings stable for 3 minutes).
5. Record all observations in tabular form.

**Sample Data Table**:

| Run | $V$ (V) | $I$ (A) | $T_{s}$ (°C) | $T_{\infty}$ (°C) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 20 | 0.8 | 45 | 30 |
| 2 | 30 | 1.4 | 60 | 30 |
| 3 | 40 | 2.0 | 75 | 31 |

---

**Part (b) — [7 Marks]**

**Given**:
- $D = 0.05$ m, $L = 0.6$ m
- $T_{s} = 80$ °C = **353 K**
- $T_{\infty} = 30$ °C = **303 K**
- $Q = 120$ W (since radiation neglected, all goes to convection)
- $k_{f} = 0.028$ W/m·K

**Step 1 — Heat transfer area**:
$$A = \pi \cdot D \cdot L = \pi \times 0.05 \times 0.6 = 0.0942 \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Temperature difference**:
$$\Delta T = 80 - 30 = 50 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 — Convective heat transfer coefficient**:
$$h_{c} = \dfrac{Q}{A \cdot \Delta T} = \dfrac{120}{0.0942 \times 50} = 25.48 \text{ W/m}^{2}\text{·K}$$

**Step 4 — Nusselt Number**:
$$Nu = \dfrac{h_{c} \cdot D}{k_{f}} = \dfrac{25.48 \times 0.05}{0.028} = 45.5$$

**Step 5 — Grashof Number** (assuming $T_{f} = (353+303)/2 = 328$ K):
$$\beta = 1/328 = 3.049 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$
$$\nu \approx 1.7 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{2}/\text{s}$$
$$Gr = \dfrac{9.81 \times 3.049 \times 10^{-3} \times 50 \times (0.05)^{3}}{(1.7 \times 10^{-5})^{2}}$$
$$Gr = \dfrac{9.81 \times 3.049 \times 10^{-3} \times 50 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4}}{2.89 \times 10^{-10}}$$
$$Gr = \dfrac{1.870 \times 10^{-4}}{2.89 \times 10^{-10}} = 6.47 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step 6 — Rayleigh Number**:
$$Ra = Gr \cdot Pr = 6.47 \times 10^{5} \times 0.71 = 4.59 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step 7 — Regime identification**:
$$Ra = 4.59 \times 10^{5} \in [10^{4}, 10^{7}] \Rightarrow \textbf{Laminar}$$

**Step 8 — Theoretical Nusselt Number** (horizontal cylinder, laminar):
$$Nu_{th} = 0.53 \cdot Ra^{1/4} = 0.53 \times (4.59 \times 10^{5})^{0.25}$$
$$Nu_{th} = 0.53 \times 25.97 = 13.76$$

**Step 9 — Percentage Error**:
$$\% \text{ Error} = \dfrac{Nu_{exp} - Nu_{th}}{Nu_{th}} \times 100 = \dfrac{45.5 - 13.76}{13.76} \times 100 = 230.6\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**:
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Forgetting to convert °C to K for the film temperature calculation. ($T_{f}$ must be in Kelvin for $\beta = 1/T_{f}$).
> - **Common Mistake 2**: Using $L$ (length) instead of $D$ (diameter) as the characteristic length in $Gr$ and $Nu$ for a **horizontal cylinder**. This causes wrong answer by orders of magnitude.
> - **Common Mistake 3**: Neglecting radiation loss in lab data. In the experiment, $\sim 15-25\%$ of input heat may be lost as radiation. Always include $Q_{rad}$ in the energy balance.
> - **Common Mistake 4**: Reporting $Nu$ without computing $Ra$ to identify the regime. Examiners deduct 1-2 marks for this.
> - **Common Mistake 5**: Using the wrong correlation constant (mixing up vertical and horizontal cylinder equations).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the Grashof Number starting from the ratio of buoyancy to viscous forces. State the physical significance of each term.

**(b) [7 Marks]** During a natural convection experiment on a vertical plate of height 0.4 m, the following data was obtained: surface temperature = 90 °C, ambient temperature = 30 °C, heat input = 80 W, plate width = 0.1 m. Determine: (i) the convective heat transfer coefficient, (ii) the Nusselt Number, (iii) whether the flow is laminar or turbulent. Take $k_{f} = 0.030$ W/m·K, $\nu = 1.6 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $Pr = 0.71$.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — [7 Marks]** (See SECTION 3.1 for the full derivation. Examiner allocates:
- 2 marks: stating buoyancy and viscous force expressions
- 2 marks: setting up the ratio and substituting Boussinesq approximation
- 2 marks: final expression with explanation of each term
- 1 mark: physical significance

**Summary of physical significance**:
- $g$ — gravitational acceleration; the external body force driving the flow.
- $\beta$ — how strongly the fluid expands per unit temperature rise.
- $\Delta T$ — the potential that drives the convection.
- $L^{3}$ — buoyancy is a **volumetric** phenomenon (scales with $L^{3}$).
- $\nu^{2}$ — viscous effects oppose the flow; stronger viscosity → weaker convection.

**Part (b) — [7 Marks]**

**Given**:
- $L = 0.4$ m (characteristic length = plate height for vertical plate)
- $W = 0.1$ m (width)
- $T_{s} = 90$ °C = **363 K**
- $T_{\infty} = 30$ °C = **303 K**
- $Q = 80$ W
- $k_{f} = 0.030$ W/m·K
- $\nu = 1.6 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s
- $Pr = 0.71$

**Step 1 — Film Temperature**:
$$T_{f} = \dfrac{363 + 303}{2} = 333 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — $\beta$ and $\Delta T$**:
$$\beta = 1/333 = 3.003 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$
$$\Delta T = 90 - 30 = 60 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 — Heat transfer area** (both sides of plate):
$$A = L \times W = 0.4 \times 0.1 = 0.04 \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Convective heat transfer coefficient**:
$$h_{c} = \dfrac{80}{0.04 \times 60} = 33.33 \text{ W/m}^{2}\text{·K}$$

**Step 5 — Nusselt Number**:
$$Nu = \dfrac{33.33 \times 0.4}{0.030} = 444.4$$

**Step 6 — Grashof Number**:
$$Gr = \dfrac{9.81 \times 3.003 \times 10^{-3} \times 60 \times (0.4)^{3}}{(1.6 \times 10^{-5})^{2}}$$

Numerator:
$$9.81 \times 3.003 \times 10^{-3} \times 60 \times 0.064 = 1.133$$
Denominator:
$$(1.6 \times 10^{-5})^{2} = 2.56 \times 10^{-10}$$
$$Gr = \dfrac{1.133}{2.56 \times 10^{-10}} = 4.43 \times 10^{9}$$

**Step 7 — Rayleigh Number**:
$$Ra = 4.43 \times 10^{9} \times 0.71 = 3.14 \times 10^{9}$$

**Step 8 — Regime identification**:
$$Ra = 3.14 \times 10^{9} > 10^{9} \Rightarrow \textbf{Turbulent regime}$$

**Step 9 — Theoretical Nusselt Number** (vertical plate, turbulent):
$$Nu_{th} = 0.10 \cdot Ra^{1/3} = 0.10 \times (3.14 \times 10^{9})^{0.333}$$
$$Ra^{1/3} = 10^{9.497 \times 0.333} = 10^{3.166} = 1466$$
$$Nu_{th} = 146.6$$

**Step 10 — Comments**:
- The experimental $Nu$ (444) is about 3× the theoretical value, suggesting significant **heat losses not accounted for**, instrument errors, or boundary effects.
- In a well-designed experiment, error should be **within ±15%**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways KTU Students Lose Marks in Natural Convection Problems**:
> 1. **Temperature unit errors**: Forgetting to convert °C to K when computing $T_{f}$ and $\beta$.
> 2. **Wrong characteristic length**: Using $L$ (length) for horizontal cylinder correlations instead of $D$ (diameter). For vertical plates, $L$ = height.
> 3. **Missing radiation correction**: Ignoring $Q_{rad} = \sigma A (T_{s}^{4} - T_{\infty}^{4})$ in real experimental data. This can cause 15-30% error.
> 4. **No regime identification**: Plugging into any correlation without first computing $Ra$ and identifying laminar/transition/turbulent.
> 5. **No steady-state confirmation**: In viva, students often fail to explain why a 15-20 min wait is needed (to allow boundary layer to fully develop and temperatures to stabilize).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Your Lab Exam!**

### **Core Concepts**
- ☐ Natural convection = buoyancy-driven flow, **no external agency**.
- ☐ Newton's Law of Cooling: $q = h_{c} A \Delta T$.
- ☐ Three governing numbers: **Nu, Gr, Pr** (or **Ra = Gr·Pr**).
- ☐ Nusselt Number: ratio of convective to conductive heat transfer.
- ☐ Grashof Number: ratio of buoyancy to viscous forces.
- ☐ Prandtl Number: property of the fluid (≈ 0.71 for air).
- ☐ For air, $\beta = 1/T_{f}$ (Boussinesq approximation).

### **Key Formulas (Memorize!)**
- ☐ $Gr = \dfrac{g \beta \Delta T L^{3}}{\nu^{2}}$
- ☐ $Ra = Gr \cdot Pr$
- ☐ $Nu = \dfrac{h_{c} L}{k_{f}}$
- ☐ $h_{c} = \dfrac{Q_{conv}}{A \Delta T}$
- ☐ $T_{f} = \dfrac{T_{s} + T_{\infty}}{2}$ (in **Kelvin**)
- ☐ $Q_{rad} = \sigma A (T_{s}^{4} - T_{\infty}^{4})$, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴

### **Correlations (Horizontal Cylinder)**
- ☐ Laminar ($10^{4} < Ra < 10^{7}$): $Nu = 0.53 \cdot Ra^{1/4}$
- ☐ Transition ($10^{7} < Ra < 10^{9}$): $Nu = 0.13 \cdot Ra^{1/3}$
- ☐ Turbulent ($10^{9} < Ra < 10^{12}$): $Nu = 0.10 \cdot Ra^{1/3}$

### **Correlations (Vertical Plate)**
- ☐ Laminar ($Ra < 10^{9}$): $Nu = 0.59 \cdot Ra^{1/4}$
- ☐ Turbulent ($Ra > 10^{9}$): $Nu = 0.10 \cdot Ra^{1/3}$

### **Lab Procedure Steps**
- ☐ Switch ON → wait 15-20 min for steady state.
- ☐ Vary heater input in 4-5 steps.
- ☐ At each step, record $V$, $I$, $T_{s}$, $T_{\infty}$.
- ☐ Compute $h_{c}$ → $Nu_{exp}$.
- ☐ Compute $Gr$, $Pr$, $Ra$ → identify regime.
- ☐ Apply correlation → $Nu_{th}$.
- ☐ Compare and report **percentage error**.

### **Common Viva Questions**
- ☐ Why is natural convection important in reactor safety?
- ☐ What is the Boussinesq approximation?
- ☐ What would happen if we used water instead of air?
- ☐ Why does the boundary layer thicken as we move along the cylinder?
- ☐ How is radiation heat loss computed in the lab?

---

**All the best for your KTU Heat Transfer Lab Exam!** 🎓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Transfer by Forced Convection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHL408 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Heat Transfer by Forced Convection # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Forced Convection** is the mode of heat transfer between a solid surface and a fluid (liquid or gas) in which the bulk fluid motion is *externally induced* by mechanical means such as a pump, blower, fan, or compressor. Unlike natural (free) convection, where fluid motion arises solely from buoyancy forces caused by density differences, forced convection dominates the heat transfer process whenever an external pressure gradient drives the flow across or through the heated/cooled surface. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech **PCCHL408 – Heat Transfer Operations Lab**, Module Experiment 2 is designed to experimentally determine the **convective heat transfer coefficient (h)** for a fluid (typically air) flowing over a heated test section (a bare metal tube, finned tube, or flat plate) under steady-state forced convection conditions, and to validate the experimentally obtained Nusselt number with standard empirical correlations. Mathematically, forced convection is governed by **Newton's Law of Cooling**: $$Q = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$ where **Q** is the rate of heat transfer (W), **h** is the convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K), **A** is the heat transfer area (m²), and **ΔT** is the temperature difference between the surface and the bulk fluid (K or °C). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Definition (verbatim style):** > "Forced convection refers to heat transfer between a surface and a fluid where the fluid motion is sustained by an external agency. The governing dimensionless parameters are the **Nusselt Number (Nu)**, **Reynolds Number (Re)**, and **Prandtl Number (Pr)**, which are experimentally correlated as $\text{Nu} = f(\text{Re}, \text{Pr})$." ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are sipping hot tea. If you simply leave the cup on a table, the tea cools slowly — this is **natural convection**, where the warm air above the tea rises gently on its own. Now, if you blow across the surface of the tea with your mouth, the tea cools dramatically faster. Your breath is acting as the **external agency** forcing the fluid (air) past the hot surface. This is **forced convection** — the same physics, but the fluid is being *pushed* rather than drifting by buoyancy. A more engineering-friendly analogy: think of a **car radiator**. The engine coolant (hot water) flows through tubes while an electric fan (or the motion of the car) forces ambient air across the radiator fins. Without the fan or the car's motion, the cooling would be far less effective. The faster the fan blows, the higher the **h** becomes, and the more heat the radiator dumps into the atmosphere. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following standard values are frequently used in forced convection calculations in the lab: | Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit | |----------|--------|---------------|------| | Stefan-Boltzmann constant | σ | 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ | W/m²·K⁴ | | Acceleration due to gravity | g | 9.81 | m/s² | | Standard atmospheric pressure | P_atm | 101.325 | kPa | | Specific heat of air (approx.) | c_p | 1005 | J/kg·K | | Thermal conductivity of air (approx.) | k | 0.026 | W/m·K | | Kinematic viscosity of air (approx.) | ν | 1.5 × 10⁻⁵ | m²/s | > [!NOTE] > In KTU 2024 lab viva questions, examiners frequently test whether students can recall the typical order of magnitude of **h** for forced convection with air, which lies in the range **10 – 200 W/m²·K**, as opposed to natural convection (5 – 25 W/m²·K) and condensation/boiling (1000 – 100000 W/m²·K). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: The Mechanism of Forced Convection When a fluid is forced over a heated surface, the following physical sequence occurs: 1. **Thermal Boundary Layer Formation:** A thin layer of fluid adjacent to the surface experiences a steep temperature gradient. The thickness of this layer (δ_t) grows as the fluid moves downstream from the leading edge. 2. **Hydrodynamic Boundary Layer:** Simultaneously, a velocity boundary layer develops due to the no-slip condition at the wall. 3. **Thermal Diffusivity Interaction:** The ratio of velocity boundary layer thickness to thermal boundary layer thickness is governed by the **Prandtl Number (Pr)**. 4. **Heat Transfer by Conduction + Convection:** At the wall, heat is transferred purely by conduction through the stagnant fluid film. Away from the wall, the bulk fluid carries heat by macroscopic motion (advection). 5. **Steady-State Energy Balance:** Under steady conditions, the rate of heat supplied to the test section (electrical heater input) equals the convective heat loss to the flowing fluid (after accounting for radiation and conduction losses). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Forced Convection Dominates Engineering Practice:** > Because the fluid velocity (and hence Reynolds number) can be externally controlled, h can be increased by an order of magnitude compared to natural convection. This makes forced convection the **workhorse** in heat exchangers, condensers, nuclear reactor cores, electronic cooling, and HVAC systems. ## 2.2 Governing Dimensionless Numbers Forced convection correlations are universally expressed in terms of three dimensionless groups: - **Reynolds Number (Re):** Ratio of inertial to viscous forces. $$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu} = \frac{v \cdot D}{\nu}$$ - **Prandtl Number (Pr):** Ratio of momentum diffusivity to thermal diffusivity. $$\text{Pr} = \frac{\mu \cdot c_p}{k} = \frac{\nu}{\alpha}$$ - **Nusselt Number (Nu):** Ratio of convective to conductive heat transfer across the fluid. $$\text{Nu} = \frac{h \cdot D}{k}$$ The **Peclet Number (Pe)** is the product of Re and Pr, representing the ratio of advective to diffusive thermal transport. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Expression | Physical Meaning | Units | Validity / Application | |---|----------------------|------------------|-------|------------------------| | 1 | $Q = h A \Delta T$ | Newton's Law of Cooling | W | Universal for convection | | 2 | $Q = \dot{m} c_p (T_{out} - T_{in})$ | Heat gained by fluid (energy balance) | W | Steady-state, no phase change | | 3 | $Q = V I$ (electrical) | Heat input from heater | W | Direct from ammeter/voltmeter | | 4 | $h = \frac{Q}{A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}}$ | Experimental h (with LMTD) | W/m²·K | Most accurate lab form | | 5 | $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log Mean Temperature Difference | K or °C | For entire test section | | 6 | $\text{Nu} = \frac{h D}{k}$ | Nusselt number (dimensionless) | – | Define h from correlation | | 7 | $\text{Re} = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (dimensionless) | – | Determines flow regime | | 8 | $\text{Pr} = \frac{\mu c_p}{k}$ | Prandtl number (dimensionless) | – | Fluid property | | 9 | $\text{Nu} = 0.023 \, \text{Re}^{0.8} \, \text{Pr}^{n}$ | **Dittus-Boelter** (turbulent, internal flow) | – | Re > 10000, 0.7 < Pr < 160; n = 0.4 (heating), 0.3 (cooling) | | 10 | $\text{Nu} = 0.027 \, \text{Re}^{0.8} \, \text{Pr}^{1/3} (\mu/\mu_w)^{0.14}$ | **Sieder-Tate** (viscous correction) | – | Better accuracy near wall | | 11 | $\text{Nu} = 0.193 \, \text{Re}^{0.618} \, \text{Pr}^{1/3}$ | Forced conv. over **flat plate** (turbulent) | – | Re_x > 5×10⁵, local Nu | | 12 | $\text{Nu}_D = C \text{Re}_D^m \text{Pr}^{1/3}$ | General cylinder correlation (e.g., Hilpert, Churchill-Bernstein) | – | External cross-flow | > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Board Insight:** The lab exam often tests whether the student can choose between *Dittus-Boelter* (internal flow) and *Hilpert / Churchill-Bernstein* (external cross-flow over cylinder). The two have very different Re exponents (~0.8 vs 0.4–0.6). Confusing them is the single most common KTU lab report error. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Forced Convection Forced convection is the cornerstone of nearly every thermal management system: - **Chemical Process Industries:** Shell-and-tube heat exchangers, jacketed reactors, condensers, reboilers. - **Power Generation:** Steam generator tubes, condenser tubes in thermal/nuclear power plants, economizers. - **HVAC & Refrigeration:** Finned-coil air-handling units, evaporator/condenser coils. - **Electronics Cooling:** Forced air cooling of CPU heat sinks, GPU arrays, power electronics. - **Aerospace:** Re-entry vehicle thermal protection, jet engine blade cooling. - **Automotive:** Engine cooling jackets, oil coolers, radiators, intercoolers. The lab experiment directly mirrors an industrial problem: predicting or validating the heat duty of a single tube under cross-flow of air, the same configuration found in air-cooled condensers and finned heat exchangers. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: The Energy Balance Used in the Lab The governing principle of the experiment is **conservation of energy** on the test section. Let us derive the working equation step by step. ### Step 1 — Heat Supplied to the Test Section A cartridge heater (or band heater) delivers electrical energy to a metal test section. Ignoring minor conduction losses through the supports, all electrical energy enters the test section: $$Q_{in} = V \cdot I$$ where **V** is the voltage (V) and **I** is the current (A) measured on the heater supply. ### Step 2 — Heat Lost to the Surroundings At steady state, the heater energy is split into three streams: 1. Convective heat transfer to the flowing fluid (desired term). 2. Radiation loss from the outer surface to the surroundings. 3. Conduction loss through the end supports. If the experimental setup is well-insulated, radiation and conduction losses are lumped into a small correction factor (typically 5–15 % of Q_in). For the *idealized* derivation, we set: $$Q_{in} = Q_{conv} + Q_{rad} + Q_{cond} \approx Q_{conv} \cdot (1 + \text{loss factor})$$ ### Step 3 — Heat Gained by the Fluid (Energy Balance on Air Stream) As the air sweeps across the heated surface, its enthalpy increases: $$Q_{conv} = \dot{m} \cdot c_{p,air} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$ where: - $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate of air (kg/s) = ρ_air × A_duct × v - $T_{in}$ = air temperature at the inlet of the test section (°C) - $T_{out}$ = air temperature at the outlet of the test section (°C) ### Step 4 — Definition of the LMTD The fluid temperature rises from T_in to T_out along the length of the test section, while the surface temperature of the tube (T_s) is nearly constant (high thermal conductivity metal, low Biot number). The driving temperature difference varies as: - At inlet: $\Delta T_1 = T_s - T_{in}$ - At outlet: $\Delta T_2 = T_s - T_{out}$ Integrating the differential form of Newton's law of cooling along the length L yields the **Logarithmic Mean Temperature Difference**: $$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}$$ > [!NOTE] > If $\Delta T_1 \approx \Delta T_2$ (within 5 %), the **arithmetic mean** is acceptable: $\Delta T_{AM} = (\Delta T_1 + \Delta T_2)/2$. KTU board answers must clearly state *why* LMTD is preferred over AMTD. ### Step 5 — Solving for the Experimental h Combining Newton's law of cooling and the LMTD expression: $$Q_{conv} = h \cdot A_s \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$$ Solving for **h**: $$h_{exp} = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})}{A_s \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}}$$ ### Step 6 — Nusselt Number (Dimensionless Heat Transfer Coefficient) To compare with empirical correlations, we non-dimensionalize h: $$\text{Nu}_{exp} = \frac{h_{exp} \cdot D}{k_{air}}$$ where **D** is the characteristic length (tube outer diameter for cross-flow) and **k_air** is evaluated at the **mean film temperature**: $$T_{film} = \frac{T_s + T_{air,mean}}{2}$$ ### Step 7 — Calculation of Reynolds Number For cross-flow over a cylinder: $$\text{Re}_D = \frac{\rho_{air} \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu_{air}}$$ All air properties are evaluated at the **mean bulk air temperature**: $$T_{bulk} = \frac{T_{in} + T_{out}}{2}$$ ### Step 8 — Comparison with Empirical Correlation For external cross-flow over a cylinder, the **Hilpert correlation** is widely used: $$\text{Nu}_D = C \cdot \text{Re}_D^m \cdot \text{Pr}^{1/3}$$ with constants **C** and **m** depending on the Re_D range: | Re_D range | C | m | |-----------|---|---| | 0.4 – 4 | 0.989 | 0.330 | | 4 – 40 | 0.911 | 0.385 | | 40 – 4000 | 0.683 | 0.466 | | 4000 – 40000 | 0.193 | 0.618 | | 40000 – 400000 | 0.027 | 0.805 | The **percent deviation** between experimental and theoretical Nu is computed as: $$\%\text{ Deviation} = \frac{\text{Nu}_{exp} - \text{Nu}_{theo}}{\text{Nu}_{theo}} \times 100$$ ## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style) > **Given Data (typical):** > - Tube outer diameter: D = 0.038 m > - Tube length (test section): L = 0.30 m > - Heater voltage: V = 70 V > - Heater current: I = 1.5 A > - Air velocity (measured by anemometer): v = 4.0 m/s > - Inlet air temperature: T_in = 32 °C > - Outlet air temperature: T_out = 58 °C > - Surface temperature (average): T_s = 95 °C > - Ambient temperature: T_∞ = 30 °C > - Air properties at bulk mean (T_bulk = 45 °C): > - ρ = 1.112 kg/m³ > - c_p = 1006 J/kg·K > - k = 0.0274 W/m·K > - μ = 1.93 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s > - Pr = 0.71 **Step 1: Heat Input** $$Q_{in} = V \times I = 70 \times 1.5 = 105 \text{ W}$$ **Step 2: Mass Flow Rate of Air (Duct cross-section 0.05 m × 0.05 m)** $$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A_{duct} \cdot v = 1.112 \times (0.05 \times 0.05) \times 4.0 = 0.01112 \text{ kg/s}$$ **Step 3: Heat Gained by Air** $$Q_{conv} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in}) = 0.01112 \times 1006 \times (58 - 32)$$ $$Q_{conv} = 0.01112 \times 1006 \times 26 = 290.9 \text{ W}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Discrepancy Alert:** Q_conv > Q_in here is physically impossible. In the real lab, the surface temperature would be lower (e.g., 80 °C) so that Q_in ≥ Q_conv. This shows the importance of *steady state* — readings must be taken only after the system stabilizes. **Step 4: Recompute with corrected T_s = 80 °C** $$\Delta T_1 = T_s - T_{in} = 80 - 32 = 48 \text{ °C}$$ $$\Delta T_2 = T_s - T_{out} = 80 - 58 = 22 \text{ °C}$$ $$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{48 - 22}{\ln(48/22)} = \frac{26}{\ln(2.1818)} = \frac{26}{0.7803} = 33.32 \text{ °C}$$ **Step 5: Heat Transfer Area** $$A_s = \pi \cdot D \cdot L = \pi \times 0.038 \times 0.30 = 0.0358 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 6: Experimental h** $$h_{exp} = \frac{\dot{m} c_p (T_{out} - T_{in})}{A_s \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}} = \frac{290.9}{0.0358 \times 33.32} = 243.9 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$ **Step 7: Reynolds Number** $$\text{Re}_D = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{1.112 \times 4.0 \times 0.038}{1.93 \times 10^{-5}} = 8755$$ **Step 8: Theoretical Nusselt Number (Hilpert, Re in 4000–40000 → C = 0.193, m = 0.618)** $$\text{Nu}_{theo} = 0.193 \times (8755)^{0.618} \times (0.71)^{1/3}$$ Computing step-by-step: $$(8755)^{0.618} = e^{0.618 \times \ln(8755)} = e^{0.618 \times 9.077} = e^{5.610} = 273.4$$ $$(0.71)^{1/3} = 0.8923$$ $$\text{Nu}_{theo} = 0.193 \times 273.4 \times 0.8923 = 47.09$$ **Step 9: Experimental Nusselt Number** $$\text{Nu}_{exp} = \frac{h_{exp} \cdot D}{k} = \frac{243.9 \times 0.038}{0.0274} = 338.2$$ **Step 10: Percent Deviation** $$\%\text{ Deviation} = \frac{338.2 - 47.09}{47.09} \times 100 = 618\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board Insight:** Such a large deviation typically signals one of three errors: (a) air velocity probe mis-calibrated, (b) thermocouples not making proper contact, or (c) tube surface temperature measured at the wrong location. The KTU examiner expects you to *comment on* the deviation and suggest corrections, not merely compute it. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Experimental Setup — Block-Level Functional Architecture The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of the forced convection test rig, showing all major components, energy streams, and instrumentation paths. ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Three Phase Variac
Voltage Regulator] -->|V increases| A2[Heater Coil
Cartridge Type] A2 -->|Q conduction| A3[Test Section
Bare Metal Tube] A3 -->|Q convection| A4[Air Stream
Forced Flow] A4 -->|Heated exhaust| A5[Outlet Plenum] A1 -->|V signal| M1[Voltmeter] A2 -->|I signal| M2[Ammeter] A3 -->|T surface| M3[Surface TC
5 nos along length] A4 -->|T in, T out| M4[Air TCs
Inlet and Outlet] B1[Blower Motor
Centrifugal Fan] -->|Drives| A4 B1 -->|Speed signal| M5[Anemometer
at Duct Inlet] A5 -->|Exhaust| B2[Atmosphere] M1 --> D1[Data Acquisition
Module] M2 --> D1 M3 --> D1 M4 --> D1 M5 --> D1 D1 --> D2[Calculation
Workbook] D2 --> D3[Result
h experimental and Nu] D3 --> D4[Validation
vs Empirical Correlation] ``` ## 4.2 Heat Transfer Pathway — Energy Flow Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Electrical Energy
V x I] -->|Heater input| P2[Metal Test Section
Stores Q] P2 -->|Conduction
Through wall| P3[Outer Surface
at T sub s] P3 -->|Forced Convection
Q conv| P4[Bulk Air Stream
at T bulk] P3 -->|Radiation
Q rad| P5[Surroundings
at T inf] P2 -->|Conduction loss
Q cond| P6[End Supports
Insulated ideally] P4 -->|Carried downstream| P7[Exhaust to Atmosphere] style P1 fill:#FFD700,color:#000 style P3 fill:#FF6347,color:#FFF style P4 fill:#87CEEB,color:#000 style P7 fill:#90EE90,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Procedural Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Step 1: Inspect Rig and Check Water Levels] --> S2[Step 2: Switch on Blower and Set Low Air Velocity] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Switch on Heater via Variac] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Set Heater Voltage and Wait for Steady State] S4 --> S5{Steady State
Reached?} S5 -->|No, T still drifting| S4 S5 -->|Yes, all T constant 5 min| S6[Step 5: Record V, I, T surface, T air in and out, v] S6 --> S7[Step 6: Increase Air Velocity and Repeat] S7 --> S8[Step 7: Repeat for 4 to 6 Velocities] S8 --> S9[Step 8: Switch Off Heater, Then Blower] S9 --> S10[Step 9: Tabulate and Calculate h, Nu, Re, Pr] S10 --> S11[Step 10: Plot Nu vs Re and Compare with Hilpert] ``` ## 4.4 Boundary Layer Development Diagram (Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR L1[Leading Edge
of Test Section] -->|X increases| L2[Hydrodynamic BL
delta sub v grows] L2 --> L3[Thermal BL
delta sub t grows] L3 --> L4[Combined Boundary Layer
Reaches Fully Developed State] L4 --> L5[Turbulent Transition
if Re exceeds critical] L5 --> L6[Exit
Boundary Layer Fully Developed] F1[Free Stream
T inf, v inf] -.->|T gradient| L2 F1 -.->|T gradient| L3 L6 -.->|Mixed Cup T| F2[Bulk Air Temperature T bulk] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper] **Define forced convection. Mention two differences between forced and natural convection.** **Model Answer:** Forced convection is the mode of heat transfer between a solid surface and a fluid in which the fluid motion is *externally induced* by mechanical means such as a pump, blower, or compressor. | Feature | Forced Convection | Natural Convection | |---------|-------------------|---------------------| | Driving force | External pressure gradient (pump, fan) | Buoyancy (density difference) | | Heat transfer coefficient (h) | High (10 – 500 W/m²·K) | Low (5 – 25 W/m²·K) | | Fluid velocity | Controlled, high (typically 1–30 m/s) | Low (~0.1–1 m/s) | | Governing correlation | Nu ∝ Reⁿ Pr^(1/3) with n ≈ 0.4–0.8 | Nu ∝ (Gr·Pr)^n | ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper] **What is the significance of the Nusselt number in forced convection?** **Model Answer:** The Nusselt number (Nu = hD/k) is the ratio of *convective* to *conductive* heat transfer across a fluid layer. It is dimensionless and represents the **enhancement** of heat transfer due to fluid motion over pure conduction. When Nu = 1, heat transfer is purely by conduction (stagnant fluid). When Nu >> 1, convection is dominant. In forced convection design, Nu is correlated with Re and Pr (e.g., Nu = 0.023 Re^0.8 Pr^0.4) to predict the convective heat transfer coefficient **h** for any given fluid velocity and geometry. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a)** *Derive an expression for the experimental convective heat transfer coefficient 'h' for a fluid flowing over a heated test section under forced convection. State clearly all assumptions made. (7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Assumptions:** 1. Steady-state operation. 2. Constant fluid properties (evaluated at mean bulk temperature). 3. One-dimensional heat flow. 4. Heat losses by radiation and end-conduction are negligible (or lumped into a small correction). 5. The test section is a long cylinder in cross-flow of air. **Derivation:** Let the test section be a horizontal cylinder of diameter **D** and length **L**, heated electrically. Air is forced across it at velocity **v**. **Step 1 — Energy Supplied by Heater:** $$Q_{in} = V \cdot I$$ **Step 2 — Heat Gained by the Air Stream** (energy balance on air): $$Q_{conv} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$ where $\dot{m} = \rho A_{duct} v$. **Step 3 — Temperature Profile Along Test Section:** At any position x along the cylinder, the local air temperature is T(x), and the surface temperature T_s is approximately uniform (high k of metal). The driving temperature difference: $$\Delta T(x) = T_s - T(x)$$ **Step 4 — Differential Form of Newton's Law:** For an elemental length dx, the heat transferred is: $$dQ = h \cdot (\pi D \cdot dx) \cdot \Delta T(x)$$ The same heat raises the air temperature: $$dQ = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot dT$$ Equating and integrating from x = 0 (inlet, ΔT₁ = T_s − T_in) to x = L (outlet, ΔT₂ = T_s − T_out): $$\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -\frac{h \pi D}{\dot{m} c_p} \int_0^L dx$$ $$\ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = -\frac{h \pi D L}{\dot{m} c_p}$$ $$h = \frac{\dot{m} c_p}{\pi D L} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{T_s - T_{in}}{T_s - T_{out}}\right)$$ **Step 5 — Expressing as LMTD:** Since LMTD is defined as: $$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$$ We can rewrite h as: $$h = \frac{\dot{m} c_p (T_{out} - T_{in})}{A_s \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}}$$ where $A_s = \pi D L$. **(b)** *In a forced convection experiment, air at 35 °C flows across a heated tube of 40 mm diameter at a velocity of 3.5 m/s. The outlet air temperature is 62 °C and the average surface temperature is 88 °C. Calculate: (i) the experimental heat transfer coefficient, (ii) the Nusselt number, and (iii) the theoretical Nusselt number using the Hilpert correlation. Comment on the deviation. (7 Marks)* *Given: Air properties at T_bulk = (35+62)/2 = 48.5 °C: ρ = 1.10 kg/m³, c_p = 1006 J/kg·K, k = 0.0276 W/m·K, μ = 1.96 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s, Pr = 0.71. Duct cross-section = 0.06 m × 0.06 m.* **Model Solution:** **(i) Experimental h:** Mass flow rate: $$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A_{duct} \cdot v = 1.10 \times 0.0036 \times 3.5 = 0.01386 \text{ kg/s}$$ Heat gained by air: $$Q = \dot{m} c_p (T_{out} - T_{in}) = 0.01386 \times 1006 \times 27 = 376.5 \text{ W}$$ LMTD: $$\Delta T_1 = 88 - 35 = 53 \text{ °C}, \quad \Delta T_2 = 88 - 62 = 26 \text{ °C}$$ $$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{53 - 26}{\ln(53/26)} = \frac{27}{0.7126} = 37.89 \text{ °C}$$ Heat transfer area (per unit length, L = 1 m taken): $$A_s = \pi \times 0.040 \times 1 = 0.1257 \text{ m}^2$$ $$h_{exp} = \frac{376.5}{0.1257 \times 37.89} = 79.05 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$ **(ii) Experimental Nusselt number:** $$\text{Nu}_{exp} = \frac{h_{exp} \cdot D}{k} = \frac{79.05 \times 0.040}{0.0276} = 114.6$$ **(iii) Theoretical Nusselt number (Hilpert):** Reynolds number: $$\text{Re}_D = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{1.10 \times 3.5 \times 0.040}{1.96 \times 10^{-5}} = 7857$$ For Re_D in the range 4000 – 40000, Hilpert constants: C = 0.193, m = 0.618. $$\text{Nu}_{theo} = 0.193 \times (7857)^{0.618} \times (0.71)^{1/3}$$ $$(7857)^{0.618} = e^{0.618 \times \ln(7857)} = e^{0.618 \times 8.969} = e^{5.543} = 254.3$$ $$\text{Nu}_{theo} = 0.193 \times 254.3 \times 0.8923 = 43.79$$ **Percent Deviation:** $$\%\text{ Deviation} = \frac{114.6 - 43.79}{43.79} \times 100 = 161.7\%$$ **Comments on Deviation:** Such a large positive deviation indicates experimental errors such as: 1. Heat loss through the tube ends not accounted for (it is large relative to the small ΔT of the air stream). 2. Thermocouple placement errors (surface TCs may not be exactly at the heated zone). 3. Air velocity measured at a single point in the duct, not the average. 4. Radiation losses from the test section, neglected in the calculation. 5. The assumption of uniform T_s along the tube length is violated due to end effects. --- ### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** *Explain the physical significance of Reynolds number, Prandtl number, and Nusselt number. State the empirical correlation used for forced convection in a tube. (7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Reynolds Number (Re):** Represents the ratio of *inertial forces* to *viscous forces* in the flow. It determines whether the flow is laminar (Re < 2300), transitional (2300 < Re < 10000), or turbulent (Re > 10000). It controls the thickness of the hydrodynamic boundary layer and hence the velocity profile near the wall. $$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu}$$ **Prandtl Number (Pr):** Represents the ratio of *momentum diffusivity* to *thermal diffusivity* of the fluid. It is a pure fluid property and indicates the relative growth rates of the velocity and thermal boundary layers. - Pr << 1 (e.g., liquid metals): thermal BL much thicker than velocity BL. - Pr ≈ 1 (e.g., gases): both BLs grow at similar rates. - Pr >> 1 (e.g., oils): thermal BL much thinner than velocity BL. $$\text{Pr} = \frac{\mu c_p}{k} = \frac{\nu}{\alpha}$$ **Nusselt Number (Nu):** Represents the ratio of *convective* heat transfer at the surface to *conductive* heat transfer through a stagnant fluid layer of the same thickness. It is the key dimensionless output of any forced convection analysis. $$\text{Nu} = \frac{h D}{k}$$ **Empirical Correlation for Forced Convection in a Tube (Internal Flow):** For fully developed turbulent flow in a smooth circular tube, the **Dittus-Boelter equation** is most commonly used: $$\text{Nu} = 0.023 \, \text{Re}^{0.8} \, \text{Pr}^n$$ where n = 0.4 when the fluid is being heated and n = 0.3 when the fluid is being cooled. Valid for: Re > 10000, 0.7 < Pr < 160, and L/D > 10. **(b)** *In a laboratory experiment on forced convection over a flat plate, the following data were obtained. The plate is maintained at 90 °C, ambient air is at 30 °C, and the air velocity is 5 m/s. The plate is 0.5 m long and 0.3 m wide. The heat lost from the plate is found to be 320 W. Calculate: (i) the experimental heat transfer coefficient, (ii) the experimental Nusselt number, and (iii) compare with the theoretical Nusselt number using the local flat-plate correlation. (7 Marks)* *Given: Air properties at film T = (90+30)/2 = 60 °C: k = 0.029 W/m·K, Pr = 0.71, ν = 1.89 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s.* **Model Solution:** **(i) Experimental h:** Heat transfer area: $$A = L \times W = 0.5 \times 0.3 = 0.15 \text{ m}^2$$ Using Newton's law: $$h_{exp} = \frac{Q}{A \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{320}{0.15 \times (90 - 30)} = \frac{320}{9.0} = 35.56 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$ **(ii) Experimental Nusselt number:** For a flat plate, the characteristic length is the plate length L = 0.5 m: $$\text{Nu}_{exp} = \frac{h_{exp} \cdot L}{k} = \frac{35.56 \times 0.5}{0.029} = 613.1$$ **(iii) Theoretical Nusselt number (Turbulent flat plate):** Reynolds number at trailing edge: $$\text{Re}_L = \frac{v L}{\nu} = \frac{5 \times 0.5}{1.89 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.323 \times 10^5$$ For Re_L > 5 × 10⁴ (turbulent), the average Nusselt number is: $$\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.037 \, \text{Re}_L^{0.8} \, \text{Pr}^{1/3}$$ $$(1.323 \times 10^5)^{0.8} = e^{0.8 \times \ln(132300)} = e^{0.8 \times 11.793} = e^{9.434} = 12550$$ $$\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.037 \times 12550 \times (0.71)^{1/3} = 0.037 \times 12550 \times 0.8923 = 414.3$$ **Percent Deviation:** $$\%\text{ Deviation} = \frac{613.1 - 414.3}{414.3} \times 100 = 48.0\%$$ A deviation of ~48 % is acceptable for a student lab (typical range is 10–50 %). The over-prediction is mainly due to: 1. Use of arithmetic ΔT (60 K) instead of LMTD (which is needed because the plate has a non-uniform T due to cooling downstream). 2. Radiation losses from the plate surface (significant at 90 °C, ~10–15 % of total). 3. Free-stream turbulence in the lab room not matching the ideal uniform flow assumed in the correlation. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark Deductions in Forced Convection Lab Viva & Reports (KTU 2024):** > 1. **Wrong characteristic length:** Using tube *inner* diameter for cross-flow over a cylinder (should be **outer** diameter D_o). *(-2 marks)* > 2. **Confusing Dittus-Boelter (internal) with Hilpert (external):** Dittus-Boelter is for **flow inside** tubes, not cross-flow. *(-3 marks)* > 3. **Air properties at wrong temperature:** Always evaluate properties at the **mean bulk** or **film** temperature and explicitly state it. *(-2 marks)* > 4. **Forgetting to include LMTD derivation** in the theory section. *(-4 marks)* > 5. **No comment on percent deviation** between experimental and theoretical Nu. The examiner expects at least 2–3 lines of *qualitative* discussion of possible error sources. *(-3 marks)* > 6. **Missing units in the final table** or inconsistent significant figures. *(-1 mark per occurrence)* > 7. **Not plotting Nu vs Re on log-log axes** to extract the exponent — this is a standard 14-mark question. *(-5 marks)* --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU 2024 ESE / Lab Viva:** - **Newton's Law of Cooling:** $Q = h A \Delta T$ — the foundation of all convection analysis. - **Three Pillars of Forced Convection Correlations:** **Re, Pr, Nu**. Always cite all three in any derivation. - **LMTD vs AMTD:** LMTD is **mandatory** when the surface temperature varies along the flow direction; use AMTD only when $\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2 < 1.05$. - **Hilpert Correlation** (external cross-flow over cylinder): - Re < 4: C = 0.989, m = 0.330 - 4 – 40: C = 0.911, m = 0.385 - 40 – 4000: C = 0.683, m = 0.466 - **4000 – 40000: C = 0.193, m = 0.618** *(most common lab range)* - 40000 – 400000: C = 0.027, m = 0.805 - **Dittus-Boelter** (internal turbulent flow in tubes): $\text{Nu} = 0.023 \text{Re}^{0.8} \text{Pr}^n$ with n = 0.4 (heating) / 0.3 (cooling). - **Sieder-Tate** (improved accuracy with viscosity correction): $\text{Nu} = 0.027 \text{Re}^{0.8} \text{Pr}^{1/3} (\mu/\mu_w)^{0.14}$. - **Flat-plate correlations:** - Laminar ($\text{Re}_L < 5 \times 10^5$): $\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.664 \text{Re}_L^{0.5} \text{Pr}^{1/3}$ - Turbulent ($\text{Re}_L > 5 \times 10^5$): $\overline{\text{Nu}}_L = 0.037 \text{Re}_L^{0.8} \text{Pr}^{1/3}$ - **Typical h values for air:** 10–200 W/m²·K (forced), 5–25 W/m²·K (natural), 50–20000 W/m²·K (with phase change). - **Air property evaluation temperatures:** Bulk mean $T_b = (T_{in}+T_{out})/2$ for Re, Pr; Film $T_f = (T_s + T_b)/2$ for k, μ, c_p in the Nusselt number. - **Steady-state criterion:** All temperatures must remain constant for at least **5 minutes** before recording readings. - **Energy balance check:** $Q_{heater} \approx Q_{air} + Q_{rad} + Q_{loss}$. If $Q_{heater} < Q_{air}$, the system is **not** at steady state — wait longer. - **Units discipline:** All quantities in SI; k in W/m·K, h in W/m²·K, μ in Pa·s, ν in m²/s, ρ in kg/m³. - **LMTD formula:** $\Delta T_{LMTD} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)/\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$ — note that $\Delta T_1$ must be the *larger* end difference. - **Percent deviation reporting:** Always report with **± sign** and state whether the experimental value *over* or *under* predicts. - **Plot of Nu vs Re on log-log axes** yields a straight line of slope **m** and intercept **log(C Pr^(1/3))** — extracting m experimentally is a frequent lab exercise.

topics.title: Thermal Conductivity of Metal Rod
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL408 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Thermal Conductivity of Metal Rod

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Thermal Conductivity (k)** of a solid material is defined as the rate of heat transfer through a unit thickness of the material per unit area per unit temperature difference, expressed in accordance with **Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction**.

The experiment under Module 2 of PCCHL408 aims to experimentally determine the **thermal conductivity of a metal rod (typically brass, copper, or aluminium)** under steady-state, one-dimensional heat conduction conditions. The metal rod is subjected to a known heat flux at one end (via an electrical heater) and is rejected at the other end via a **water-cooled heat exchanger**, with the lateral surface of the rod heavily insulated to enforce unidirectional heat flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment validates **Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction** in practical laboratory conditions. The metal rod functions as a controlled, one-dimensional conduction pathway, while the cooling water circuit acts as the **calorimetric reference** for measuring the actual heat transmitted through the rod.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Working Principle:** At steady-state, the temperature at any point in the rod becomes invariant with time. Under this condition, the rate of heat flow through every cross-section of the rod is **constant and equal to the heat picked up by the cooling water**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding one end of a long metal spoon over a lit candle flame.

- After a few seconds, the **end you are holding becomes warm**, even though it is far from the flame.
- This is because heat "travels" through the metal.
- If the spoon were made of **copper**, the heat reaches your hand faster than if it were made of **stainless steel** — because copper has a **higher thermal conductivity**.
- **Thermal Conductivity (k)** is, in simple terms, a material's "**efficiency of passing heat**."

In the lab, instead of waiting for your hand to feel the heat, we:
1. **Force a known quantity of heat** into one end of the rod using an electrical heater.
2. **Measure the temperature drop** along a known length of the rod.
3. **Measure how much heat the cooling water removes** at the other end.
4. Use these values to **calculate k** mathematically.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Memory Trick:** Think of **k** as the *diameter of a pipe carrying heat*. A large k means a "wide pipe" — heat flows easily. A small k means a "narrow pipe" — heat struggles to pass. The temperature gradient is the *pressure difference* pushing heat through.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Magnitude (Brass) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thermal Conductivity | k | **109 – 120** | W/(m·K) |
| Specific Heat of Water | Cw | **4.186** | kJ/(kg·K) |
| Stefan-Boltzmann Constant | σ | **5.67 × 10⁻⁸** | W/(m²·K⁴) |
| Emissivity (polished brass) | ε | **0.05 – 0.10** | dimensionless |
| Standard Room Temperature | T_∞ | **25 – 30** | °C |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction

For a one-dimensional, steady-state conduction problem with no internal heat generation, **Fourier's Law** states:

$$q_x = -k \cdot A \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}$$

Where:
- $q_x$ = heat transfer rate in the x-direction (W)
- $k$ = thermal conductivity of the material (W/m·K)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area perpendicular to heat flow (m²)
- $\frac{dT}{dx}$ = temperature gradient in the direction of heat flow (K/m)

The **negative sign** signifies that heat flows in the direction of **decreasing temperature** (Second Law of Thermodynamics — spontaneous heat transfer from hot to cold).

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Experiment

1. **Heat Input Stage:** An electrical heating coil wound around one end of the rod supplies a controlled quantity of heat, $Q_{in} = V \times I$ (W).
2. **Steady-State Condition:** The heater is run for a sufficient duration (typically 60–90 minutes) until the temperature at every thermocouple point along the rod stabilizes. At steady state: $\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = 0$.
3. **Unidirectional Enforcement:** The lateral surface of the rod is wrapped with thick insulation (asbestos rope or glass wool) to minimize radial heat losses, ensuring $q_{radial} \approx 0$.
4. **Temperature Profile Acquisition:** Six (or more) thermocouples $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4, T_5, T_6$ measure the axial temperature distribution.
5. **Calorimetric Reference:** Cooling water flowing through the jacket at the cold end absorbs the heat. Measuring the **inlet temperature ($T_{c,in}$)**, **outlet temperature ($T_{c,out}$)**, and the **mass flow rate ($m_w$)** provides the actual heat transmitted: $Q_{water} = \dot{m}_w \cdot C_{p,w} \cdot (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$.
6. **Heat Loss Compensation:** Although insulation is provided, residual surface heat losses occur by:
   - **Natural Convection:** $Q_{conv} = h_c \cdot A_s \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)$
   - **Thermal Radiation:** $Q_{rad} = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A_s \cdot (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)$
7. **Net Heat Transmitted:** $Q_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{loss} \approx Q_{water}$
8. **Final Calculation:** Apply Fourier's Law over a segment with measured $\Delta T$ and $L$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet / High-Yield Equation Bank

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = k \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T}{\Delta x}$ | Fourier's Law (integrated form) | W |
| 2 | $Q_{in} = V \times I$ | Electrical heat input | W |
| 3 | $Q_{water} = \dot{m}_w \cdot C_{p,w} \cdot (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$ | Heat absorbed by cooling water | W |
| 4 | $\dot{m}_w = \rho_w \cdot Q_v = \dfrac{\rho_w \cdot V_{collected}}{t}$ | Mass flow rate of water | kg/s |
| 5 | $k = \dfrac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta T}$ | Thermal conductivity (working equation) | W/(m·K) |
| 6 | $Q_{conv} = h_c \cdot A_s \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)$ | Convective heat loss (per segment) | W |
| 7 | $Q_{rad} = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A_s \cdot (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)$ | Radiative heat loss (per segment) | W |
| 8 | $h_c = 1.42 \cdot \left(\dfrac{\Delta T}{L}\right)^{0.25}$ | Natural convection on horizontal cylinder (McAdams) | W/(m²·K) |
| 9 | $T_s(K) = T_s(°C) + 273.15$ | Absolute temperature conversion | K |
| 10 | $A_s = \pi \cdot d \cdot L_{segment}$ | Lateral surface area of a segment | m² |
| 11 | $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2$ | Cross-sectional area of rod | m² |

> [!WARNING]
> **KRITICAL KTU PITFALL:** All temperatures used in radiation heat loss calculations (Eq. 7) **MUST be in Kelvin**. Using °C in Eq. 7 is the most common mistake costing 2-3 marks per question.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Heat Exchanger Design:** Selecting tube materials for boilers, condensers, and shell-and-tube heat exchangers.
- **Electronics Cooling:** Choosing heat sink materials (aluminium vs. copper) for CPUs and power electronics.
- **Building Insulation:** Distinguishing conductive materials (metal) from insulative materials (wood, foam).
- **Turbine Blade Cooling:** Internal cooling channels in jet engine blades.
- **Refractory Selection:** Furnace linings in metallurgical industries.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Working Equation for Thermal Conductivity

### Starting from Fourier's Law

For one-dimensional, steady-state conduction in a metal rod of uniform cross-section $A$ and constant thermal conductivity $k$:

$$Q = -k \cdot A \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}$$

### Step 1: Separate Variables

$$Q \cdot dx = -k \cdot A \cdot dT$$

### Step 2: Apply Boundary Conditions

Integrate from $x = x_1$ (temperature $T_1$) to $x = x_2$ (temperature $T_2$):

$$\int_{x_1}^{x_2} Q \, dx = -k \cdot A \cdot \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT$$

### Step 3: Execute Integration

$$Q \cdot (x_2 - x_1) = -k \cdot A \cdot (T_2 - T_1) = k \cdot A \cdot (T_1 - T_2)$$

### Step 4: Rearrange for k

$$\boxed{k = \frac{Q \cdot (x_2 - x_1)}{A \cdot (T_1 - T_2)} = \frac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta T}}$$

Where $L = x_2 - x_1$ is the segment length and $\Delta T = T_1 - T_2$ is the temperature drop.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Heat Loss by Natural Convection (Horizontal Cylinder)

The McAdams correlation for natural convection from a horizontal cylinder in laminar flow is:

$$Nu_D = \frac{h_c \cdot D}{k_{air}} = C \cdot (Gr \cdot Pr)^{n}$$

For $10^4 < Gr \cdot Pr < 10^9$ (laminar regime):

$$C = 0.53, \quad n = 0.25$$

Solving for $h_c$:

$$h_c = 0.53 \cdot \frac{k_{air}}{D} \cdot (Gr \cdot Pr)^{0.25}$$

A simplified textbook form used in KTU labs is:

$$h_c = 1.42 \cdot \left(\frac{\Delta T}{L}\right)^{0.25} \quad \text{[W/m²K]}$$

Where $\Delta T = T_s - T_\infty$ and $L$ is the characteristic length of the cylinder.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Heat Loss by Radiation

Using the Stefan-Boltzmann Law for a small surface in a large enclosure:

$$Q_{rad} = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A_s \cdot (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)$$

### Step 1: Convert Temperatures to Kelvin

$$T_s(K) = T_s(°C) + 273.15$$
$$T_\infty(K) = T_\infty(°C) + 273.15$$

### Step 2: Apply the Factorization Identity

Using the identity $a^4 - b^4 = (a^2 + b^2)(a + b)(a - b)$:

$$T_s^4 - T_\infty^4 = (T_s^2 + T_\infty^2)(T_s + T_\infty)(T_s - T_\infty)$$

### Step 3: Substitute

$$Q_{rad} = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A_s \cdot (T_s^2 + T_\infty^2)(T_s + T_\infty)(T_s - T_\infty)$$

### Step 4: Approximation (Linearized Form)

For small $\Delta T$ relative to $T_\infty$, the linearized coefficient is:

$$h_{rad} = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot (T_s + T_\infty)(T_s^2 + T_\infty^2)$$

$$Q_{rad} = h_{rad} \cdot A_s \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU ESE Pattern)

**Given:**
- Brass rod: diameter $d = 38$ mm, total length = 0.5 m
- Heater voltage $V = 100$ V, current $I = 1.5$ A
- Cooling water flow rate: 500 mL collected in 60 seconds
- Inlet water temperature: 28 °C
- Outlet water temperature: 34 °C
- Temperatures along rod: $T_1 = 95°C$, $T_2 = 85°C$, $T_3 = 75°C$ at distances $x_1 = 0.05$ m, $x_2 = 0.15$ m, $x_3 = 0.25$ m
- Ambient temperature: 30 °C
- Emissivity of brass: $\varepsilon = 0.05$

**Find:** Thermal conductivity of the brass rod.

---

### Solution

**Step 1: Calculate cross-sectional area**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.038)^2 = 1.134 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2: Calculate heat input**

$$Q_{in} = V \times I = 100 \times 1.5 = 150 \, \text{W}$$

**Step 3: Calculate mass flow rate of cooling water**

Volume collected $V_w = 500$ mL $= 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³
Density of water $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³

$$\dot{m}_w = \frac{\rho_w \cdot V_w}{t} = \frac{1000 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}}{60} = 8.33 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{kg/s}$$

**Step 4: Calculate heat absorbed by water**

$$Q_{water} = \dot{m}_w \cdot C_{p,w} \cdot (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}) = 8.33 \times 10^{-3} \times 4186 \times (34 - 28)$$

$$Q_{water} = 209.2 \, \text{W}$$

**Step 5: Heat Loss Adjustment**

Since insulation is not perfect, we use the water-side calorimetric value as $Q_{net}$:

$$Q_{net} = Q_{water} = 209.2 \, \text{W}$$

*(In a real lab, the heater-input 150 W will not equal water-pickup 209 W due to losses. KTU typically instructs students to use $Q = \dot{m}_w C_{p,w} \Delta T_w$ as the actual transmitted heat.)*

**Step 6: Apply Fourier's Law Between $T_1$ and $T_2$**

$$L = 0.15 - 0.05 = 0.10 \, \text{m}$$
$$\Delta T = 95 - 85 = 10 \, \text{K}$$

$$k = \frac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{209.2 \times 0.10}{1.134 \times 10^{-3} \times 10}$$

$$\boxed{k = \frac{20.92}{0.01134} = 1844 \, \text{W/m·K}}$$

**Interpretation:** The result is higher than the standard brass value (109 W/m·K) because the actual heat transmitted is overestimated. In practice, k should be computed using $Q_{net} = Q_{water} - Q_{loss}$ where $Q_{loss}$ is calculated for each segment using the $h_c$ and $h_{rad}$ values.

**Step 7: Heat Loss for One Segment (e.g., $T_1 = 95°C = 368$ K, $T_\infty = 30°C = 303$ K)**

Surface area of segment $A_s = \pi \cdot d \cdot L = \pi \cdot 0.038 \cdot 0.10 = 0.01194$ m²

**Convection loss:**

$$h_c = 1.42 \cdot \left(\frac{95 - 30}{0.038}\right)^{0.25} = 1.42 \cdot (1710.5)^{0.25} = 1.42 \cdot 6.45 = 9.16 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

$$Q_{conv} = 9.16 \times 0.01194 \times (95 - 30) = 7.11 \, \text{W}$$

**Radiation loss:**

$$Q_{rad} = 0.05 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.01194 \times (368^4 - 303^4)$$

$$Q_{rad} = 3.385 \times 10^{-9} \times (1.832 \times 10^{10} - 8.43 \times 10^{9})$$

$$Q_{rad} = 3.385 \times 10^{-9} \times 9.89 \times 10^{9} = 33.5 \, \text{W}$$

**Total segment loss:**

$$Q_{loss} = Q_{conv} + Q_{rad} = 7.11 + 33.5 = 40.61 \, \text{W}$$

**Step 8: Recalculate k with corrected Q**

$$Q_{corrected} = Q_{water} - Q_{loss,total} = 209.2 - 40.61 = 168.6 \, \text{W}$$

$$k_{corrected} = \frac{168.6 \times 0.10}{1.134 \times 10^{-3} \times 10} = 1486 \, \text{W/m·K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The deviation from the standard value persists due to simplified correlations used in the lab. KTU typically accepts an experimental error band of **±20%** as valid.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Apparatus Layout (Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC Variac Power Supply] -->|V volts| B[Heater Coil Wound on Rod End]
    B -->|Q_in = V x I| C[Hot End of Metal Rod]
    C -->|Conduction through rod| D[Insulated Lateral Surface of Rod]
    D -.->|Q_loss by conv and radiation| E[Ambient Air Surroundings]
    C -->|1D axial conduction| F[Cooling Water Jacket at Cold End]
    F -->|Q_water picked up| G[Outlet Water T_c_out]
    H[Inlet Water T_c_in] --> F
    I[Thermocouple Cluster T1 T2 T3 T4 T5 T6] -.->|Temperature profile| C
    J[Voltmeter and Ammeter] -.->|Monitors Q_in| B
    K[Rotameter and Measuring Cylinder] -.->|Measures m_dot_w| F
```

---

## 4.2 Steady-State Heat Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    stepA[Heater Input Q_in] --> stepB[Conduction Along Rod Body]
    stepB --> stepC{Steady State Reached?}
    stepC -->|No| stepD[Wait for Temperature Stabilization]
    stepD --> stepC
    stepC -->|Yes| stepE[Record T1 T2 T3 T4 T5 T6]
    stepE --> stepF[Record T_c_in and T_c_out]
    stepF --> stepG[Measure Water Mass Flow Rate]
    stepG --> stepH[Calculate Q_water]
    stepH --> stepI[Calculate Q_loss per Segment]
    stepI --> stepJ[Compute Q_net]
    stepJ --> stepK[Apply Fourier Law to Get k]
    stepK --> stepL[Compare with Standard Value and Compute Percent Error]
```

---

## 4.3 Temperature Distribution Along Rod (Conceptual Profile)

```mermaid
graph TB
    P1["Hot End T1 = 95 C"] --> P2["Section 1 T2 = 85 C"]
    P2 --> P3["Section 2 T3 = 75 C"]
    P3 --> P4["Section 3 T4 = 65 C"]
    P4 --> P5["Section 4 T5 = 55 C"]
    P5 --> P6["Section 5 T6 = 45 C"]
    P6 --> P7["Cold End with Water Jacket T_c = 34 C"]
    noteA["Temperature drops linearly if k is constant and no losses"] -.- P1
    noteB["Real profile curves slightly downward due to radial losses"] -.- P3
```

---

## 4.4 Heat Balance Sankey Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Heater Q_in = 150 W] --> B[Conduction through Rod Q_cond = 168 W]
    A -.->|Q_loss = 40 W| C[Radial Heat Loss to Ambient]
    B --> D[Water Pickup Q_water = 209 W]
    D --> E[Outlet Water Carries Heat Away]
```

---

## 4.5 Physical Schematic Description (For Lab Record)

The apparatus consists of a **horizontal metal rod** (typically brass) of circular cross-section, mounted on a sturdy metal frame. The rod is encased in a **thick layer of insulation** (asbestos or glass wool) along its lateral surface. At the **hot end**, a **nichrome heating element** is wound tightly around the rod, connected to a **variac** for voltage regulation. At the **cold end**, a **brass water jacket** is soldered, through which a controlled flow of water is maintained from a **constant-head overhead tank**. Six (or more) **calibrated thermocouples** are inserted radially at fixed intervals (typically 50 mm apart) into small drilled holes on the rod. A **voltmeter and ammeter** are wired in parallel and series with the heater respectively. A **rotameter** measures the water flow rate.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction and explain the physical significance of the negative sign.**

**Model Answer:**
Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction states that the rate of heat transfer through a homogeneous solid is directly proportional to the cross-sectional area perpendicular to heat flow and the temperature gradient along the direction of flow.

$$Q = -k \cdot A \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}$$

**Physical significance of the negative sign:** The negative sign indicates that heat flows in the direction of **decreasing temperature** — i.e., from a region of higher temperature to a region of lower temperature, in accordance with the Second Law of Thermodynamics. Without the negative sign, the equation would incorrectly predict heat flow from cold to hot.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the necessity of insulation in the thermal conductivity of metal rod experiment?**

**Model Answer:**
The lateral surface of the metal rod is wrapped with a thick layer of insulation (asbestos rope / glass wool) to **minimize radial heat losses** to the surroundings. If insulation is not provided, heat would escape in the radial direction by natural convection and radiation, violating the assumption of **one-dimensional axial heat flow**. This would make the actual heat conducted through the rod **less than the heat supplied by the heater**, leading to a significant underestimation of thermal conductivity.

---

## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the working equation used for the determination of thermal conductivity of a metal rod from first principles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A brass rod of 40 mm diameter and 600 mm length is heated at one end by an electric heater. At steady state, the following data were recorded:

- Heater input: 110 V, 1.2 A
- Water flow rate: 600 mL/min
- Inlet water temperature: 28 °C; Outlet water temperature: 35 °C
