> The KTU-typical expected $T$ vs. $t$ curve shows: (1) a flat plateau at $T_i$ during heating, (2) a small downward kink during transfer, (3) a smooth exponential decay, and (4) an asymptotic flattening near $T_\infty$. Any sharp discontinuity or linear segment indicates experimental error.

## 4.4 Heat Flow Pathways in a Cooling Sphere

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cold Water T infinity] --> B[Convective Film Resistance 1 over h As]
    B --> C[Solid Conduction Resistance ln r2 over r1 over 4 pi k L]
    C --> D[Thermal Mass rho V cp Storage]
    D --> E[Equilibrium after 4 to 5 tau]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **lumped regime**, the conduction resistance is negligible (because $\text{Bi} < 0.1$ implies $h$ is small OR $k$ is large). The thermal mass becomes the bottleneck and decays exponentially.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define thermal diffusivity. What are its units? Mention its significance in unsteady state heat transfer.**

**Model Answer:**
Thermal diffusivity $\alpha$ is defined as the ratio of thermal conductivity to the volumetric heat capacity of a material:

$$\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho \, c_p} \quad \left[ \text{Units: } \text{m}^2/\text{s} \right]$$

It represents the **rate at which temperature changes propagate through a material**. A high $\alpha$ means the material responds quickly to thermal disturbances (e.g., metals like silver, copper, aluminum). A low $\alpha$ means slow propagation (e.g., plastics, wood, insulators). It is the central property governing all unsteady state conduction phenomena.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the lumped capacitance method? Under what condition is it applicable?**

**Model Answer:**
The **lumped capacitance method** is a simplified approach to unsteady state heat transfer that assumes the temperature inside a body is **spatially uniform** at any instant. The body's entire thermal capacity is treated as a single "lumped" node. The transient response is governed by a single ODE whose solution is an exponential decay:

$$\frac{T - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = e^{-t/\tau}, \qquad \tau = \frac{\rho V c_p}{h A_s}$$

**Applicability condition:** The method is valid only when the **Biot number is less than 0.1**, i.e., $\text{Bi} = \dfrac{h L_c}{k} < 0.1$. This ensures that the internal conduction resistance is much smaller than the surface convective resistance, so spatial temperature gradients inside the body are negligible.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Major Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert all temperatures to **absolute units (K)** before computing differences.
> 2. Using the wrong **characteristic length** — for a sphere it is $D/6$, not $D/2$.
> 3. Reporting thermal diffusivity with the wrong sign (must be positive).
> 4. Mixing up the Fo number (uses $L_c^{\,2}$) with the time constant $\tau$ (uses $V/A_s$).
> 5. Failing to check the **Bi validity criterion** before applying lumped analysis — examiners specifically deduct 2 marks for this.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2, ESE]`
> **(a)** A brass sphere of diameter **5 cm**, initially at a uniform temperature of **80 °C**, is suddenly immersed in a large bath of water maintained at **25 °C**. The convective heat transfer coefficient is **h = 250 W/m²K**. The properties of brass are: $k = 110 \, \text{W/mK}$, $\rho = 8530 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 380 \, \text{J/kgK}$. Compute:
> 1. The Biot number and verify the applicability of the lumped capacitance method.
> 2. The time required for the sphere to cool to **45 °C**.
>
> **(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the Heisler chart procedure for determining the transient temperature distribution in a solid of finite geometry. How is thermal diffusivity extracted from the experimental data?

#### Part (a) Model Solution — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Compute the characteristic length for a sphere.**

$$L_c = \frac{D}{6} = \frac{0.05}{6} = 8.333 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}$$

**Step 2: Compute the Biot number.**

$$\text{Bi} = \frac{h \, L_c}{k} = \frac{250 \times 8.333 \times 10^{-3}}{110} = 0.0189$$

Since $\text{Bi} = 0.0189 < 0.1$, the **lumped capacitance method is VALID**. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3: Compute the volume and surface area per unit volume-to-area ratio (which for a sphere equals $D/6$ as already found).**

$$\frac{V}{A_s} = \frac{D}{6} = 8.333 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}$$

**Step 4: Compute the thermal time constant.**

$$\tau = \frac{\rho \, V \, c_p}{h \, A_s} = \frac{\rho \, c_p \, (V/A_s)}{h} = \frac{8530 \times 380 \times 8.333 \times 10^{-3}}{250}$$

$$\tau = \frac{8530 \times 380 \times 8.333 \times 10^{-3}}{250} = \frac{27008.5}{250} = 108.03 \, \text{s}$$

**Step 5: Use the lumped solution to find the cooling time from 80 °C to 45 °C.**

$$\frac{T - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = e^{-t/\tau}$$

$$\frac{45 - 25}{80 - 25} = e^{-t/\tau}$$

$$\frac{20}{55} = 0.3636 = e^{-t/108.03}$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln(0.3636) = -\frac{t}{108.03}$$

$$-1.0116 = -\frac{t}{108.03}$$

$$t = 1.0116 \times 108.03 = 109.28 \, \text{s} \approx 1.82 \, \text{min}$$

**Answer: The sphere takes approximately 109.3 seconds (≈ 1.82 minutes) to cool to 45 °C.** **(Full 7 marks: Bi calc 2, validity 1, time-constant 2, exponential solve 2)**

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution — 7 Marks

**Heisler Chart Procedure:**

1. **Measure** the transient centerline temperature $T_0$ of the specimen at successive times $t$ during cooling (or heating) in a constant-temperature bath.
2. **Compute** the dimensionless temperature $\theta_0^* = \dfrac{T_0 - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty}$ for each time $t$.
3. **Compute** the inverse Biot number $1/\text{Bi} = \dfrac{k}{h L_c}$ from measured $h$ and known material $k$.
4. **Read** the Heisler chart for the appropriate geometry (sphere, cylinder, or slab) at the computed $1/\text{Bi}$ to obtain the coefficients $C_1$ and $\zeta_1$ of the one-term series.
5. **Take natural log** of the one-term solution:

$$\ln(\theta_0^*) = \ln C_1 - \zeta_1^{\,2} \cdot \text{Fo}$$

6. **Plot** $\ln(\theta_0^*)$ versus $t$. The slope equals $-\zeta_1^{\,2} \cdot \dfrac{\alpha}{L_c^{\,2}}$, a straight line.
7. **Extract thermal diffusivity:**

$$\alpha = -\frac{\text{slope} \times L_c^{\,2}}{\zeta_1^{\,2}}$$

8. **Compare** with literature value and compute percentage deviation.

The Heisler chart (a) is essentially a log-log plot of $\theta_0^*$ vs. Fo for fixed $1/\text{Bi}$, and (b) a plot of $C_1$ and $\zeta_1$ vs. $1/\text{Bi}$. **(Full 7 marks: procedure 3, extraction logic 2, sketch description 1, interpretation 1)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2, ESE]`
> **(a)** A thick steel slab initially at **30 °C** has one of its surfaces suddenly raised to **250 °C** and held at that temperature. Calculate the temperature at a depth of **4 cm** from the surface after **10 minutes**. Take the thermal diffusivity of steel as $\alpha = 0.6 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$. Use the error function solution.
>
> **(b)** Discuss the significance of Biot and Fourier numbers in unsteady state heat transfer with suitable examples. Why is the lumped capacitance method inappropriate when $\text{Bi} > 0.1$?

#### Part (a) Model Solution — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Identify the model.** The slab is "thick" → semi-infinite solid assumption is valid. The error-function solution applies.

$$\frac{T(x,t) - T_i}{T_s - T_i} = \text{erfc}\!\left( \frac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}} \right)$$

**Step 2: Compute the argument of the error function.**

$$\eta = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}} = \frac{0.04}{2 \sqrt{0.6 \times 10^{-5} \times 600}}$$

$$= \frac{0.04}{2 \sqrt{3.6 \times 10^{-3}}} = \frac{0.04}{2 \times 0.06} = \frac{0.04}{0.12} = 0.3333$$

**Step 3: Read the error function value from standard tables.**

$$\text{erf}(0.3333) = 0.3627 \implies \text{erfc}(0.3333) = 1 - 0.3627 = 0.6373$$

**Step 4: Compute the temperature at depth 4 cm and time 600 s.**

$$\frac{T - 30}{250 - 30} = 0.6373$$

$$T - 30 = 0.6373 \times 220 = 140.21$$

$$\boxed{T = 170.21 \, ^\circ\text{C}}$$

**Answer: The temperature at 4 cm depth after 10 minutes is approximately 170.2 °C.** **(Full 7 marks: model selection 1, argument calc 2, erfc read 2, final temp 2)**

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution — 7 Marks

**Biot Number (Bi):** Ratio of internal conduction resistance to surface convective resistance.

$$\text{Bi} = \frac{h L_c}{k}$$

- $\text{Bi} \to 0$: Convection is the bottleneck; temperature inside is uniform → lumped method works.
- $\text{Bi} \to \infty$: Conduction inside is the bottleneck; surface instantly matches fluid → semi-infinite solution works.
- $0.1 < \text{Bi} < 40$: Both resistances matter; Heisler charts needed.

**Fourier Number (Fo):** Ratio of elapsed time to characteristic thermal diffusion time of the body.

$$\text{Fo} = \frac{\alpha t}{L_c^{\,2}}$$

- $\text{Fo} \to 0$: Early time — temperature change has not penetrated fully.
- $\text{Fo} \to \infty$: Long time — body approaches steady state.

**Why lumped fails at $\text{Bi} > 0.1$:**

When $\text{Bi} > 0.1$, the surface convective resistance is small compared to internal conduction resistance. Heat entering or leaving the surface **cannot distribute fast enough** throughout the interior, so the body develops **significant spatial temperature gradients**. The assumption of "lumped" uniform temperature breaks down. Mathematically, the exponential solution $\theta^* = e^{-t/\tau}$ over-predicts the cooling rate because it ignores the slower internal equilibration, leading to errors greater than 5%. The correct solution then requires the **infinite series** of the transient conduction equation, which is captured in the Heisler chart's one-term (or higher) approximation.

**Examples:**
- Cooling of a small copper wire in air (Bi < 0.1) → lumped.
- Cooling of a large steel ingot in water (Bi > 0.1) → Heisler chart.
- Quenching of a glass plate (Bi > 1) → Heisler with multiple terms. **(Full 7 marks: Bi explained 2, Fo explained 2, lumped failure 2, examples 1)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Unsteady state** means temperature varies with both **position AND time** — the hallmark is $\partial T/\partial t \neq 0$.
- The **governing PDE** in 1D is $\partial T/\partial t = \alpha \, \partial^2 T/\partial x^2$.
- **Thermal diffusivity** $\alpha = k / (\rho c_p)$ has units of $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$; metals have $\alpha \sim 10^{-5}$, insulators have $\alpha \sim 10^{-7}$.
- The **Biot number** tells you which model to use: $\text{Bi} < 0.1$ → lumped; $0.1 \le \text{Bi} \le 40$ → Heisler; $\text{Bi} \to \infty$ → semi-infinite.
- The **Fourier number** tells you *when* a body has effectively reached steady state; $\text{Fo} > 0.2$ typically marks significant thermal penetration.
- The **lumped capacitance solution** is a single exponential decay governed by a single time constant $\tau = \rho V c_p / (h A_s)$.
- The **Heisler chart** is a graphical two-parameter family of curves; the one-term approximation is accurate for $\text{Fo} > 0.2$.
- The **semi-infinite solid solution** uses the **error function** $\text{erf}(\eta)$ and is valid only when penetration depth $\delta_p \ll $ total body thickness.
- **Characteristic lengths** (MUST memorize): Sphere $D/6$, Infinite cylinder $D/4$, Plane wall $L/2$.
- **Practical steady state** is reached after approximately **4 to 5 time constants** ($\tau$), corresponding to ~98% to 99% of $(T_i - T_\infty)$ closure.
- In the KTU lab, the typical specimen is a **brass sphere or cylinder**, the bath fluid is **water**, and the thermocouple is a **K-type or J-type** embedded at the center.
- **Most common exam trap:** computing Bi incorrectly using the wrong $L_c$ — verify the geometry before substituting.
- **Most common experimental error:** transfer lag between hot and cold bath causes an initial kink in the $T$ vs. $t$ plot; minimize by transferring in **under 2 seconds**.
- **Heisler chart extraction:** always plot $\ln(\theta_0^*)$ vs. $t$ — a straight line is the *only* check that the data is internally consistent.
- **Percentage deviation** of $\alpha$ from literature should ideally be **less than 10%**; greater deviation suggests poor convection, faulty thermocouple placement, or incorrect $h$ measurement.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Critical Radius of Insulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL408 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Critical Radius of Insulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Radius of Insulation ($r_c$)** is defined as the outer radius of insulation on a cylindrical or spherical body at which the rate of heat transfer (or heat loss) reaches a **maximum** value. Beyond this radius, increasing the insulation thickness paradoxically **decreases** the heat transfer rate, contrary to common intuition.

The mathematical condition is:

$$r_c = \frac{k}{h}$$

where:
- $k$ = thermal conductivity of the insulation material (W/m·K)
- $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient at the outer surface (W/m²·K)

> [!NOTE]
> For a **cylindrical pipe**, $r_c$ is measured from the central axis. For a **sphere**, the same relationship $r_c = k/h$ holds, but the critical radius refers to the outer surface of the sphere.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a hot chai (tea) cup on a cold, windy day. You wrap it with a woolen cover to keep it warm. Does adding **more** wool always keep the tea hotter? Surprisingly, no!

- **If the outer radius of insulation ($r_2$) is less than $r_c$**: Adding insulation actually **increases** the heat loss. The tea cools faster! This is because the increase in outer surface area (which enhances convection) dominates over the increase in conduction resistance.
- **If $r_2 = r_c$**: Heat loss is at its **maximum**. The tea cools the fastest at this exact thickness.
- **If $r_2 > r_c$**: Now the conduction resistance through the thick insulation dominates, and the tea stays warm longer.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight**: This is why electrical cables (small radius, low $r_c$) can overheat if wrapped too tightly with thick insulation. The insulation must be chosen to keep the cable radius **above** $r_c$ for safe operation.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard values are frequently encountered in KTU lab experiments:

| Parameter | Typical Range | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Thermal conductivity of insulation ($k$) | **0.03 – 0.20** | W/m·K |
| Convective heat transfer coefficient ($h$) | **5 – 50** (natural) / **50 – 500** (forced) | W/m²·K |
| Critical radius ($r_c$) | **0.001 – 0.05** | m (1 mm – 50 mm) |
| Test cylinder inner radius ($r_1$) | **0.005 – 0.025** | m |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Foundation — The Resistance Network

Heat loss from an insulated cylinder involves **two thermal resistances in series**:

1. **Conduction resistance** through the cylindrical insulation shell (from radius $r_1$ to $r_2$)
2. **Convective resistance** at the outer surface (at radius $r_2$)

> [!NOTE]
> As the outer radius $r_2$ increases, the conduction resistance **increases** (more material to conduct through), but the convective resistance **decreases** (larger surface area for convection). These two opposing effects create the maximum at $r_c = k/h$.

### 2.2 The Two Competing Effects Explained

- **Effect 1 (Increased Conduction Resistance):** A thicker insulation layer means heat has to travel a longer path, which raises $R_{cond} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}$. This tends to **reduce** heat loss.
- **Effect 2 (Reduced Convective Resistance):** A larger outer radius $r_2$ means more surface area exposed to convection, which lowers $R_{conv} = \frac{1}{h \cdot 2\pi r_2 L}$. This tends to **increase** heat loss.
- **The Crossover:** Initially, Effect 2 dominates (because $r_2$ is small), so heat loss grows. After crossing $r_c$, Effect 1 dominates and heat loss drops.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $Q = \frac{T_1 - T_\infty}{R_{total}}$ | Overall heat transfer rate | W |
| 2 | $R_{cond} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}$ | Conduction resistance (cylinder) | K/W |
| 3 | $R_{conv} = \frac{1}{h \cdot 2\pi r_2 L}$ | Convective resistance (cylinder) | W/m²·K⁻¹ |
| 4 | $R_{total} = R_{cond} + R_{conv}$ | Total resistance in series | K/W |
| 5 | $r_c = \frac{k}{h}$ | Critical radius of insulation (cylinder) | m |
| 6 | $Q_{max} = \frac{T_1 - T_\infty}{\frac{1}{k}(1) + \frac{1}{h \cdot r_c}}$ (after substitution) | Maximum heat transfer rate | W |
| 7 | $Q = \frac{T_1 - T_\infty}{\frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L} + \frac{1}{2\pi r_2 L h}}$ | Full heat loss equation (cylinder) | W |
| 8 | $r_c = \frac{2k}{h}$ | Critical radius (sphere) | m |
| 9 | $Q = \frac{4\pi k (T_1 - T_\infty)}{\frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r_2}}$ | Conduction through sphere (no convection) | W |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note: At the critical radius $r_2 = r_c = k/h$, the simplified expression becomes $Q_{max} = \frac{2\pi L (T_1 - T_\infty)}{e^{1}/(h \cdot r_c)}$, which after substitution reduces to a value independent of insulation thickness at that specific point.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Steam pipe lagging** — Choosing optimum insulation thickness for power plant steam lines to minimize losses.
- **Electrical cable design** — Preventing overheating in buried or bundled power cables.
- **Cryogenic storage** — Understanding why highly insulating materials (low $k$) give small $r_c$, requiring careful design.
- **Building insulation** — Wall insulation behaves similarly when the wall has internal heat generation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Critical Radius for a Cylindrical Pipe

Consider a long cylindrical pipe of inner radius $r_1$ at uniform temperature $T_1$, covered with insulation of outer radius $r_2$ and thermal conductivity $k$. The surrounding ambient is at $T_\infty$ with convective coefficient $h$.

**Step 1: Write the conduction resistance through the insulation shell.**

For steady-state, one-dimensional radial conduction through a cylindrical shell:

$$Q_{cond} = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{\frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}}$$

So the conduction resistance is:

$$R_{cond} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}$$

**Step 2: Write the convection resistance at the outer surface.**

$$Q_{conv} = h \cdot (2\pi r_2 L) \cdot (T_2 - T_\infty)$$

So the convection resistance is:

$$R_{conv} = \frac{1}{h \cdot 2\pi r_2 L}$$

**Step 3: Combine the resistances in series (steady-state, no heat generation).**

$$Q = \frac{T_1 - T_\infty}{R_{cond} + R_{conv}} = \frac{2\pi L (T_1 - T_\infty)}{\frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k} + \frac{1}{h r_2}}$$

**Step 4: Differentiate $Q$ with respect to $r_2$ and set $dQ/dr_2 = 0$.**

The numerator is constant. So to maximize $Q$, we must **minimize the denominator** $f(r_2)$:

$$f(r_2) = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k} + \frac{1}{h r_2}$$

Differentiate with respect to $r_2$:

$$\frac{df}{dr_2} = \frac{1}{k r_2} - \frac{1}{h r_2^2} = 0$$

**Step 5: Solve for $r_2$.**

$$\frac{1}{k r_2} = \frac{1}{h r_2^2}$$

Multiplying both sides by $k r_2^2$:

$$r_2 = \frac{k}{h}$$

Therefore, the **critical radius of insulation** is:

$$\boxed{r_c = \frac{k}{h}}$$

**Step 6: Confirm it's a maximum (second derivative test).**

$$\frac{d^2 f}{d r_2^2} = -\frac{1}{k r_2^2} + \frac{2}{h r_2^3} = \frac{1}{r_2^2}\left(-\frac{1}{k} + \frac{2}{h r_2}\right)$$

At $r_2 = k/h$, substitute:

$$\frac{d^2 f}{d r_2^2} = \frac{1}{r_2^2}\left(-\frac{1}{k} + \frac{2}{k}\right) = \frac{1}{k r_2^2} > 0$$

Since $d^2f/dr_2^2 > 0$, the denominator $f(r_2)$ has a **minimum** at $r_c$, which means $Q$ has a **maximum**. ∎

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Model Lab Calculation)

> [!NOTE]
> The following is a complete KTU-style model calculation that mimics the lab viva and record book expectations.

**Given Data:**
- Inner radius of test cylinder: $r_1 = 0.0125$ m (12.5 mm)
- Thermal conductivity of insulation: $k = 0.055$ W/m·K
- Heated length: $L = 0.30$ m
- Inner surface temperature: $T_1 = 423$ K (≈ 150 °C)
- Ambient temperature: $T_\infty = 303$ K (30 °C)
- Heater input: $V = 50$ V, $I = 1.2$ A

**Step 1: Calculate heat input from the heater.**

$$Q_{input} = V \times I = 50 \times 1.2 = 60 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the critical radius.**

$$r_c = \frac{k}{h}$$

We need to determine $h$ experimentally. Suppose at $r_2 = 0.025$ m (bare cylinder with no insulation), we find $h = 11$ W/m²·K from the relation:

$$Q = h \cdot 2\pi r_1 L (T_1 - T_\infty)$$

$$h = \frac{Q}{2\pi r_1 L (T_1 - T_\infty)} = \frac{60}{2\pi \times 0.0125 \times 0.30 \times (423 - 303)}$$

$$h = \frac{60}{2\pi \times 0.0125 \times 0.30 \times 120} = \frac{60}{2.827} \approx 21.22 \text{ W/m²·K}$$

Wait — recalculating more carefully:

$$2\pi \times 0.0125 \times 0.30 \times 120 = 2.8274$$

$$h = \frac{60}{2.8274} \approx 21.22 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the critical radius.**

$$r_c = \frac{k}{h} = \frac{0.055}{21.22} \approx 0.00259 \text{ m} \approx 2.59 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Calculate heat loss for various outer radii and tabulate.**

Using $Q = \frac{2\pi L (T_1 - T_\infty)}{\frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k} + \frac{1}{h r_2}}$

Compute for several $r_2$ values:

| $r_2$ (mm) | $\ln(r_2/r_1)$ | $\frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k}$ | $\frac{1}{h r_2}$ | Denominator | $Q$ (W) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 12.5 | 0.000 | 0.000 | 3.770 | 3.770 | 95.5 |
| 14.0 | 0.113 | 2.057 | 3.366 | 5.423 | 66.4 |
| 16.0 | 0.247 | 4.485 | 2.946 | 7.431 | 48.4 |
| 20.0 | 0.470 | 8.545 | 2.357 | 10.902 | 33.0 |
| 25.0 | 0.693 | 12.60 | 1.886 | 14.486 | 24.8 |
| 30.0 | 0.875 | 15.91 | 1.572 | 17.482 | 20.6 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The maximum $Q$ occurs at $r_2 = 12.5$ mm (the smallest practical radius in this case), and as $r_2$ grows, $Q$ falls. This indicates that the bare inner radius $r_1$ is already **greater than** $r_c = 2.59$ mm, so any insulation added **decreases** heat loss. This is the **safe regime** for industrial piping.

### 3.3 Critical Radius for a Sphere — Quick Derivation

For a sphere, the conduction resistance is:

$$R_{cond, sphere} = \frac{1}{4\pi k}\left(\frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r_2}\right)$$

The convection resistance is:

$$R_{conv, sphere} = \frac{1}{h \cdot 4\pi r_2^2}$$

Total:

$$Q = \frac{4\pi (T_1 - T_\infty)}{\frac{1}{k}\left(\frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r_2}\right) + \frac{1}{h r_2^2}}$$

Differentiating the denominator with respect to $r_2$ and setting to zero:

$$\frac{d}{dr_2}\left[-\frac{1}{k r_2} + \frac{1}{h r_2^2}\right] = \frac{1}{k r_2^2} - \frac{2}{h r_2^3} = 0$$

Solving:

$$\boxed{r_{c, sphere} = \frac{2k}{h}}$$

Note the **factor of 2** compared to the cylinder. Spheres have a more pronounced critical radius effect due to area scaling as $r^2$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Experimental Apparatus

The diagram below maps the functional architecture of a typical KTU lab apparatus used to determine the critical radius of insulation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Supply 230V AC] --> B[Variac Autotransformer]
    B --> C[Heater Coil Inside Test Cylinder]
    C --> D[Test Cylinder r1]
    D --> E[Insulation Layer r2 variable]
    E --> F[Outer Surface Exposed to Air]
    F --> G[Air Blower for Forced Convection]
    D --> H[T1 Inner Surface Thermocouple]
    E --> I[T2 Outer Surface Thermocouple]
    J[Ambient Temperature Sensor] --> K[Digital Temperature Indicator]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    B --> L[Voltmeter V]
    B --> M[Ammeter I]
    L --> N[Data Acquisition and Record Book]
    M --> N
    K --> N
    N --> O[Calculate Q, h, and rc]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Experimental Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start: Switch on heater and blower] --> P2[Set voltage using variac to fixed V]
    P2 --> P3[Wait for steady state 30 to 45 minutes]
    P3 --> P4[Record T1, T2, Tinf, V, I]
    P4 --> P5[Calculate h from bare cylinder data]
    P5 --> P6[Add insulation of known thickness]
    P6 --> P7[Repeat readings at new r2]
    P7 --> P8[Plot Q versus r2 graph]
    P8 --> P9[Find r2 where Q is maximum equals rc]
    P9 --> P10[Compare with theoretical rc equals k by h]
    P10 --> P11[End of experiment]
```

### 4.3 Thermal Resistance Network Diagram

The resistance network visualises the series arrangement of conduction and convection paths.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[T1 hot inner surface] --> B[Conduction Resistance ln r2 by r1 over 2 pi k L]
    B --> C[T2 outer insulation surface]
    C --> D[Convection Resistance 1 over h times 2 pi r2 L]
    D --> E[Tinf ambient air]
```

> [!TIP]
> In the resistance network, if you imagine stretching the conduction block (more insulation) and shrinking the convection block (larger area), the **series sum** first decreases (Q rises) then increases (Q falls). The minimum total resistance corresponds to the maximum heat flow.

### 4.4 Apparatus List (KTU Lab Record Requirement)

| Sl. No. | Component | Specification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Test cylinder (brass/copper) | 25 mm OD, 300 mm length |
| 2 | Nichrome heater coil | 500 W capacity |
| 3 | Insulation material (asbestos rope / glass wool) | $k$ ≈ 0.04 – 0.06 W/m·K |
| 4 | Variac autotransformer | 0 – 270 V, 5 A |
| 5 | Digital temperature indicator | With K-type thermocouples |
| 6 | Air blower with speed regulator | For forced convection |
| 7 | Voltmeter, Ammeter | 0 – 300 V, 0 – 5 A |
| 8 | Measuring tape / vernier caliper | For insulation thickness |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define the term **critical radius of insulation**. State the condition under which adding insulation to a bare pipe will actually **increase** the heat loss.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The critical radius of insulation is the outer radius $r_c$ of the insulation at which the heat transfer rate from a cylinder or sphere is **maximum**. It is given by $r_c = k/h$, where $k$ is the thermal conductivity of the insulation and $h$ is the convective heat transfer coefficient.

If the bare pipe radius $r_1 < r_c$, then adding insulation in the range $r_1 < r_2 < r_c$ will **increase** heat loss because the increased surface area dominates over the increased conduction resistance. Heat loss only begins to **decrease** once $r_2 > r_c$. **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for condition explanation)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Why does the **convective resistance** at the outer surface of an insulated cylinder **decrease** as the insulation thickness increases?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The convective resistance for a cylinder is given by $R_{conv} = 1/(h \cdot 2\pi r_2 L)$. As the insulation thickness increases, the outer radius $r_2$ increases, which **increases the surface area** $A = 2\pi r_2 L$ available for convective heat transfer. Since $R_{conv}$ is inversely proportional to this area, the convective resistance **decreases**. This reduction in $R_{conv}$ competes with the rising conduction resistance, leading to the critical radius phenomenon. **(3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for area relation, 1 for inverse relationship explanation)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length ESE Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of $2\pi L$** in the denominator — KTU examiners specifically deduct **2 marks** for this.
> 2. **Wrong units in $r_c$** — Students often write $r_c$ in mm without converting; final answer must be in **metres** or with unit consistency.
> 3. **Confusing cylinder and sphere formulae** — The sphere has $r_c = 2k/h$, **not** $k/h$. A 1-mark deduction applies for missing the factor of 2.
> 4. **Skipping the second derivative test** — KTU 2024 scheme requires verification that the critical point is a maximum, not a minimum.
> 5. **In numerical problems, not computing $Q$ at multiple $r_2$** — A graph or table is expected for full marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the critical radius of insulation for a **cylindrical pipe**, clearly stating all assumptions. Show that the condition corresponds to a **maximum** in heat transfer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 50 mm diameter steam pipe is lagged with insulation having $k = 0.07$ W/m·K. The convective heat transfer coefficient on the outer surface is $h = 8.5$ W/m²·K, and the temperatures are $T_1 = 420$ K, $T_\infty = 305$ K. Calculate:
- (i) The critical radius of insulation.
- (ii) Whether the bare pipe radius is greater or less than $r_c$.
- (iii) The percentage change in heat loss when insulation thickness is increased from 0 to 20 mm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):** Use the derivation from **Section 3.1** of these notes. The key steps are:
1. Write $R_{cond} = \ln(r_2/r_1)/(2\pi k L)$ and $R_{conv} = 1/(h \cdot 2\pi r_2 L)$. **(1 mark)**
2. Total resistance in series, hence $Q = 2\pi L (T_1 - T_\infty)/[\ln(r_2/r_1)/k + 1/(h r_2)]$. **(1 mark)**
3. To maximise $Q$, minimise the denominator. **(1 mark)**
4. Differentiate $f(r_2) = \ln(r_2/r_1)/k + 1/(h r_2)$ and set to zero. **(2 marks)**
5. Arrive at $r_c = k/h$ and verify by second derivative. **(1 mark)**
6. State assumptions: steady state, constant $k$, constant $h$, no heat generation, long cylinder (1D radial). **(1 mark)**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $r_1 = 0.025$ m, $k = 0.07$ W/m·K, $h = 8.5$ W/m²·K, $T_1 = 420$ K, $T_\infty = 305$ K, $T_1 - T_\infty = 115$ K.

**(i) Critical radius:**

$$r_c = \frac{k}{h} = \frac{0.07}{8.5} = 0.00824 \text{ m} = 8.24 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Comparison:** Bare pipe radius $r_1 = 25$ mm $\gg r_c = 8.24$ mm. So $r_1 > r_c$, meaning **any additional insulation will decrease the heat loss**. **(1 mark)**

**(iii) Heat loss at $r_2 = 25$ mm (bare):**

$$Q_1 = h \cdot 2\pi r_1 L (T_1 - T_\infty) = 8.5 \times 2\pi \times 0.025 \times L \times 115$$

$$Q_1/L = 8.5 \times 0.1571 \times 115 = 153.55 \text{ W/m}$$

**Heat loss at $r_2 = 25 + 20 = 45$ mm (insulated):**

$$\frac{Q_2}{L} = \frac{2\pi (T_1 - T_\infty)}{\frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k} + \frac{1}{h r_2}}$$

$$\ln(0.045/0.025) = \ln(1.8) = 0.5878$$

$$\frac{0.5878}{0.07} + \frac{1}{8.5 \times 0.045} = 8.397 + 2.614 = 11.011$$

$$\frac{Q_2}{L} = \frac{2\pi \times 115}{11.011} = \frac{722.57}{11.011} = 65.62 \text{ W/m}$$

**Percentage change:**

$$\%\Delta Q = \frac{Q_2 - Q_1}{Q_1} \times 100 = \frac{65.62 - 153.55}{153.55} \times 100 = -57.27\%$$

Heat loss **decreases by 57.27 %** upon adding 20 mm of insulation. **(1 mark for sign and 1 mark for numerical value)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a neat **resistance network diagram**, explain why heat loss from an insulated cylinder first **increases** and then **decreases** as insulation thickness is increased. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A thin electrical cable of 5 mm diameter is to be insulated with a material of $k = 0.04$ W/m·K. The cable surface temperature is 350 K, and the surrounding air is at 300 K with $h = 10$ W/m²·K. The insulation can be applied up to a maximum outer radius of 15 mm.
- (i) Calculate the critical radius of insulation.
- (ii) Will the cable **overheat** if 10 mm thickness of insulation is applied? Justify with calculations.
- (iii) Suggest the safe insulation thickness range. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Explanation (7 marks):** Refer to **Section 4.3** (Mermaid resistance network) and **Section 2.2** (Two competing effects).
1. Draw resistance network: $R_{cond}$ in series with $R_{conv}$. **(1 mark)**
2. State $R_{cond} = \ln(r_2/r_1)/(2\pi k L)$ increases with $r_2$. **(1 mark)**
3. State $R_{conv} = 1/(h \cdot 2\pi r_2 L)$ decreases with $r_2$. **(1 mark)**
4. Total resistance $R = R_{cond} + R_{conv}$ has a minimum at $r_2 = k/h$. **(2 marks)**
5. Explain that $Q_{max}$ occurs at minimum $R$, and beyond $r_c$, $R$ grows so $Q$ falls. **(1 mark)**
6. Conclude with industrial relevance. **(1 mark)**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $r_1 = 0.0025$ m, $k = 0.04$ W/m·K, $T_1 = 350$ K, $T_\infty = 300$ K, $h = 10$ W/m²·K.

**(i) Critical radius:**

$$r_c = \frac{k}{h} = \frac{0.04}{10} = 0.004 \text{ m} = 4 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Check at $r_2 = 0.0025 + 0.010 = 0.0125$ m (12.5 mm):**

Since $r_2 = 12.5$ mm $> r_c = 4$ mm, the cable is in the **safe regime** — heat loss will **decrease** with insulation.

Verify by computing $Q$ at $r_1 = 2.5$ mm and $r_2 = 12.5$ mm:

$$\frac{Q_1}{L} = 10 \times 2\pi \times 0.0025 \times (350 - 300) = 7.854 \text{ W/m}$$

$$\ln(0.0125/0.0025) = \ln(5) = 1.6094$$

$$\frac{Q_2}{L} = \frac{2\pi \times 50}{\frac{1.6094}{0.04} + \frac{1}{10 \times 0.0125}} = \frac{314.16}{40.235 + 8.0} = \frac{314.16}{48.235} = 6.51 \text{ W/m}$$

So heat loss drops from 7.854 W/m to 6.51 W/m — a **17 % reduction**. The cable will **not** overheat. **(2 marks)**

**(iii) Safe thickness range:** Any $r_2 > r_c = 4$ mm, i.e. insulation thickness $> r_c - r_1 = 4 - 2.5 = 1.5$ mm. Recommended: thickness between **2 mm and 12.5 mm** (well within the 15 mm limit). **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Critical Radius of Insulation**

- ☐ **Critical radius formula (cylinder):** $r_c = k/h$ — memorise without fail.
- ☐ **Critical radius formula (sphere):** $r_c = 2k/h$ — note the factor of 2.
- ☐ **Physical meaning:** $r_c$ is the outer radius at which heat loss is **maximum**, not minimum.
- ☐ **Counter-intuitive zone:** For $r_1 < r_2 < r_c$, heat loss **increases** with insulation.
- ☐ **Industrial safe zone:** Keep $r_2 > r_c$ for steam pipes; keep $r_2 < r_c$ for **no purpose** (always exceed $r_c$ for safety).
- ☐ **Effect of high $h$:** Forced convection (high $h$) → small $r_c$ → cables overheat easily.
- ☐ **Effect of high $k$:** Better conductor → larger $r_c$ → safer to insulate.
- ☐ **Heat loss equation (cylinder):** $Q = 2\pi L (T_1 - T_\infty)/[\ln(r_2/r_1)/k + 1/(h r_2)]$.
- ☐ **Heat loss equation (sphere):** $Q = 4\pi (T_1 - T_\infty)/[1/k(1/r_1 - 1/r_2) + 1/(h r_2^2)]$.
- ☐ **Units check:** $k$ in W/m·K, $h$ in W/m²·K, $r_c$ in metres.
- ☐ **Lab procedure tip:** Always calculate $h$ from the bare cylinder data first, then use it to find the theoretical $r_c$ for comparison.
- ☐ **Plot required:** $Q$ vs $r_2$ graph — maximum on the curve gives experimental $r_c$.
- ☐ **Second derivative test:** Must be performed to confirm maximum (KTU 2024 marks).
- ☐ **Assumptions to state:** Steady state, constant properties, 1-D radial conduction, no heat generation, long cylinder.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Combined Convection and Radiation Heat Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL408 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Combined Convection and Radiation Heat Transfer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> **Combined Convection and Radiation Heat Transfer** refers to the simultaneous occurrence of two distinct heat transfer modes — *convection* (due to fluid motion over a heated surface) and *radiation* (due to electromagnetic emission from the surface) — from the same physical body to its surroundings. In most industrial heat exchange systems operating at moderate-to-high temperatures, both modes act in parallel and must be evaluated together to obtain the true rate of heat loss.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a hot **chai** (tea) cup freshly poured in a Kerala "tea stall." Two things happen at once:
1. **Convection** → The hot steam rises from the open surface, and cool air from the sides sweeps across the cup walls, carrying heat away (this is like a fan blowing over the cup).
2. **Radiation** → Even if you place the cup in a completely still, vacuum-like chamber (no air movement at all), the cup still loses heat by glowing infrared energy — exactly like the way a glowing red-hot iron rod feels hot from a distance without touching.

The **total cooling** of the chai is the **sum of both effects**. That is the essence of "Combined Convection and Radiation."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Physical Constants Used in This Experiment:**
> - **Stefan–Boltzmann Constant**: $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$
> - **Emissivity ($\varepsilon$)**: Surface property, $0 \leq \varepsilon \leq 1$ (1 = blackbody)
> - **Absolute Temperatures Only** for all radiation terms (always in **Kelvin**).

### 1.2 Why This Experiment Is Done in KTU Labs

A horizontal cylindrical heater (usually a **nichrome wire-wound copper pipe** carrying current) is used as the heat source. By measuring the steady-state surface temperature and ambient temperature, students quantify **how much heat leaves by convection vs. radiation**, and learn the difference between an *apparent* (overall) heat transfer coefficient and the *true* individual contributions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Breakdown of the Two Modes

**A. Convection Component:**
- Driven by a **temperature difference** between the heated surface ($T_s$) and the bulk fluid (air at $T_\infty$).
- Governed by **Newton's Law of Cooling**.
- Depends on: surface geometry, orientation, fluid properties (Pr, $\mu$, $k$, $\beta$), and $\Delta T$.

**B. Radiation Component:**
- Driven by the **fourth-power difference** between $T_s^4$ and $T_{surr}^4$ (Kelvin).
- Governed by the **Stefan–Boltzmann Law**.
- Depends on: **emissivity ($\varepsilon$)**, **view factor ($F$)**, and absolute temperatures.
- Does **not** require any intervening medium (works even in vacuum).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** *"Convection loves linear $\Delta T$; Radiation loves $T^4$."* When $T_s$ is small (near ambient), radiation is negligible. Above **400 K**, radiation starts dominating.

### 2.2 Derivation of the Combined Heat Transfer Coefficient

The total heat leaving the surface equals the **sum of the two parallel heat flows**:

$$q_{total} = q_{conv} + q_{rad}$$

Expanding each term:

$$q_{total} = h_c \, A (T_s - T_\infty) + \varepsilon \, \sigma \, A (T_s^4 - T_{surr}^4)$$

To unify the two into a single linear expression in $\Delta T$, define a **radiation heat transfer coefficient** $h_r$:

$$h_r \equiv \varepsilon \, \sigma \, (T_s^2 + T_{surr}^2)(T_s + T_{surr})$$

This comes from the algebraic identity:

$$T_s^4 - T_{surr}^4 = (T_s^2 - T_{surr}^2)(T_s^2 + T_{surr}^2) = (T_s - T_{surr})(T_s + T_{surr})(T_s^2 + T_{surr}^2)$$

Therefore:

$$q_{total} = (h_c + h_r) \, A \, (T_s - T_\infty)$$

And the **overall (combined) heat transfer coefficient** is:

$$h_{combined} = h_c + h_r$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Application in Lab** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Convective heat loss | $q_{conv} = h_c \, A \, \Delta T$ | W | Heat carried by air motion |
| Radiative heat loss | $q_{rad} = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, A \, (T_s^4 - T_{surr}^4)$ | W | Heat emitted as IR waves |
| Radiation coefficient | $h_r = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, (T_s^2 + T_{surr}^2)(T_s + T_{surr})$ | W/m²K | Linearizes the $T^4$ term |
| Combined coefficient | $h_{combined} = h_c + h_r$ | W/m²K | Equivalent single-mode coefficient |
| Total heat loss | $q_{total} = h_{combined} \, A \, (T_s - T_\infty)$ | W | Used to verify energy balance |
| Energy balance check | $V \cdot I = q_{total}$ | W | Electrical input = Total thermal output |
| Electrical input | $Q_{in} = V \cdot I$ | W | From voltmeter and ammeter readings |
| Emissivity (oxidized copper) | $\varepsilon \approx 0.6$ to $0.78$ | dimensionless | Common KTU default for the heater |
| Emissivity (polished aluminum) | $\varepsilon \approx 0.05$ to $0.1$ | dimensionless | Highly reflective surface |
| View factor (cylinder, large room) | $F \approx 1.0$ | dimensionless | Small object in large enclosure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always convert temperatures to Kelvin** before using radiation formulas. A common KTU valuation error is using °C in the $T^4$ term — this gives a wildly wrong answer.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This principle is used in designing:
- **Furnace insulation** (high $\varepsilon$ surfaces need reflective shields).
- **Reactor cooling coils** at high temperatures.
- **Spacecraft thermal control** (in vacuum, only radiation works).
- **Solar collector absorber plates**.
- **High-voltage transmission line ampacity calculations** (current-carrying conductors radiate significantly above 400 K).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From First Principles to Combined Coefficient

We start with the **first law of thermodynamics** applied to the heater in steady state. No heat is stored (temperature is constant), so:

**Electrical energy in = Total thermal energy out**

$$V \cdot I = q_{conv} + q_{rad}$$

**Step 1:** Write the convective term using Newton's law.

$$q_{conv} = h_c \, A_s \, (T_s - T_\infty)$$

**Step 2:** Write the radiative term using Stefan–Boltzmann.

$$q_{rad} = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, A_s \, (T_s^4 - T_{surr}^4)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the energy balance.

$$V \cdot I = h_c \, A_s \, (T_s - T_\infty) + \varepsilon \, \sigma \, A_s \, (T_s^4 - T_{surr}^4)$$

**Step 4:** Divide through by $A_s (T_s - T_\infty)$ to isolate coefficients.

$$\frac{V \cdot I}{A_s (T_s - T_\infty)} = h_c + \frac{\varepsilon \, \sigma \, (T_s^4 - T_{surr}^4)}{(T_s - T_\infty)}$$

**Step 5:** Use the algebraic difference-of-fourth-powers identity.

$$\frac{T_s^4 - T_{surr}^4}{T_s - T_{surr}} = (T_s + T_{surr})(T_s^2 + T_{surr}^2)$$

**Step 6:** Substitute the identity.

$$\frac{V \cdot I}{A_s (T_s - T_\infty)} = h_c + \varepsilon \, \sigma \, (T_s + T_{surr})(T_s^2 + T_{surr}^2)$$

**Step 7:** Define the right-hand term as the **radiation heat transfer coefficient**.

$$h_r \equiv \varepsilon \, \sigma \, (T_s + T_{surr})(T_s^2 + T_{surr}^2)$$

**Step 8:** Final compact result.

$$\boxed{h_{combined} = h_c + h_r = \frac{V \cdot I}{A_s \, (T_s - T_\infty)}}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example

**Problem Statement:** A horizontal cylindrical heater with surface area $A_s = 0.05 \ \text{m}^2$ is supplied with $V = 40 \ \text{V}$ and $I = 1.5 \ \text{A}$. The steady-state surface temperature is $T_s = 400 \ \text{K}$, ambient air is $T_\infty = 300 \ \text{K}$, and the surrounding enclosure walls are at $T_{surr} = 305 \ \text{K}$. The surface emissivity is $\varepsilon = 0.7$. Calculate (a) electrical input, (b) $h_r$, (c) $h_c$, (d) $h_{combined}$, and (e) the fraction of heat lost by radiation.

**Step 1 — Electrical input:**

$$Q_{in} = V \cdot I = 40 \times 1.5 = 60 \ \text{W}$$

**Step 2 — Radiation heat transfer coefficient:**

$$h_r = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, (T_s + T_{surr})(T_s^2 + T_{surr}^2)$$

Compute $T_s^2$ and $T_{surr}^2$:

$$T_s^2 = 400^2 = 160000 \ \text{K}^2$$

$$T_{surr}^2 = 305^2 = 93025 \ \text{K}^2$$

Sum of squares:

$$T_s^2 + T_{surr}^2 = 160000 + 93025 = 253025 \ \text{K}^2$$

Sum:

$$T_s + T_{surr} = 400 + 305 = 705 \ \text{K}$$

Now plug in:

$$h_r = 0.7 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 705 \times 253025$$

First, multiply the constants:

$$0.7 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} = 3.969 \times 10^{-8}$$

Multiply by $705$:

$$3.969 \times 10^{-8} \times 705 = 2.798 \times 10^{-5}$$

Multiply by $253025$:

$$2.798 \times 10^{-5} \times 253025 \approx 7.08 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 3 — Total (combined) coefficient from energy balance:**

$$h_{combined} = \frac{Q_{in}}{A_s \, (T_s - T_\infty)} = \frac{60}{0.05 \times (400 - 300)} = \frac{60}{5} = 12 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 4 — Convection coefficient:**

$$h_c = h_{combined} - h_r = 12 - 7.08 = 4.92 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 5 — Radiation fraction:**

$$f_{rad} = \frac{h_r}{h_{combined}} = \frac{7.08}{12} = 0.59 = 59\%$$

### 3.3 Final Answer Table

| **Parameter** | **Value** | **Significance** |
|---|---|---|
| Electrical input | $60 \ \text{W}$ | Energy supplied by heater |
| $h_r$ | $7.08 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ | Radiation contribution |
| $h_c$ | $4.92 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ | Convection contribution |
| $h_{combined}$ | $12 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ | Effective overall coefficient |
| Radiation fraction | $59\%$ | Confirms radiation dominance at 400 K |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Combined Heat Loss System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PWR[DC Power Supply] -->|V, I| HTR[Heated Cylinder]
    HTR -->|q_conv| AIR[Surrounding Air T_inf]
    HTR -->|q_rad| SUR[Surrounding Walls T_surr]
    HTR -->|Thermocouple| TC[T_s Measurement]
    TC --> DAT[Data Acquisition]
    AIR --> DAT
    SUR --> DAT
    DAT --> CALC[Calculation Module]
    CALC --> HC[h_c via Energy Balance]
    CALC --> HR[h_r via Stefan-Boltzmann]
    HC --> OUT[Total h_combined]
    HR --> OUT
    OUT --> FRAC[Radiation Fraction]
```

### 4.2 Heat Flow Partition (Block-Level Schematic)

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 3
    block:src["Heat Source (Q_in = V*I)"]:1
    split
        block:conv["Convection Path\nq_conv = h_c*A*ΔT"]:2
        block:rad["Radiation Path\nq_rad = ε*σ*A*(T_s⁴ − T_surr⁴)"]:2
    end
    block:sink["Ambient Sinks (Air + Walls)"]:3
    src --> conv --> sink
    src --> rad --> sink
```

### 4.3 Thermal Resistance Network (Parallel Combination)

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 3
    block:nodeT["T_s (Surface)"]:1
    block:rc["1/(h_c * A)"]:1
    block:ra["1/(h_r * A)"]:1
    block:amb["T_∞ , T_surr (Ambient)"]:3
    nodeT --> rc --> amb
    nodeT --> ra --> amb
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** The parallel-resistance layout above is the *exact thermal-circuit analogue* of the equation $h_{combined} = h_c + h_r$. Two resistances in parallel give a total conductance that is the **sum** of individual conductances — hence coefficients add, not reciprocals.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the combined heat transfer coefficient. Under what condition does radiation become negligible compared to convection?*

**Model Answer:**
The combined heat transfer coefficient $h_{combined}$ is the single effective coefficient that, when multiplied by the surface area and the linear temperature difference $(T_s - T_\infty)$, gives the total heat loss accounting for both convection and radiation.

$$h_{combined} = h_c + h_r$$

Radiation becomes negligible when the surface temperature is close to the ambient temperature (typically when $T_s < 350 \ \text{K}$), because $h_r \propto (T_s^2 + T_{surr}^2)(T_s + T_{surr})$ shrinks rapidly as temperatures fall. Also, low-emissivity ($\varepsilon \to 0$) surfaces suppress radiation entirely.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State Stefan–Boltzmann Law and explain why temperatures must be in Kelvin.*

**Model Answer:**
Stefan–Boltzmann Law states that the radiant energy emitted per unit area per unit time by a blackbody is proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature.

$$E_b = \sigma \, T^4 \quad \text{where} \quad \sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$$

Temperatures must be in Kelvin because the law is derived from thermodynamic arguments (Planck's distribution integrated over all wavelengths) that require $T = 0$ to represent the *complete absence of thermal radiation*. Using Celsius would give non-zero radiation at 0 °C, which violates the physical meaning of $T = 0 \ \text{K}$ as the absolute zero of thermal motion and emission.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** A horizontal copper cylinder of diameter $D = 0.04 \ \text{m}$ and length $L = 0.4 \ \text{m}$ is heated electrically. In steady state, the voltage is $V = 35 \ \text{V}$, current is $I = 1.2 \ \text{A}$, surface temperature $T_s = 380 \ \text{K}$, ambient air $T_\infty = 303 \ \text{K}$, and surroundings $T_{surr} = 308 \ \text{K}$. Surface emissivity $\varepsilon = 0.75$. Calculate the **radiation heat transfer coefficient**, the **combined heat transfer coefficient**, and the **percentage of heat lost by radiation**. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Surface area of cylinder.

$$A_s = \pi D L = \pi \times 0.04 \times 0.4 = 0.05027 \ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Electrical input.

$$Q_{in} = V \cdot I = 35 \times 1.2 = 42 \ \text{W}$$

**Step 3:** Radiation coefficient.

$$h_r = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, (T_s + T_{surr})(T_s^2 + T_{surr}^2)$$

$$T_s^2 = 380^2 = 144400, \quad T_{surr}^2 = 308^2 = 94864$$

$$T_s^2 + T_{surr}^2 = 239264, \quad T_s + T_{surr} = 688$$

$$h_r = 0.75 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 688 \times 239264$$

$$h_r = 4.2525 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.6473 \times 10^{8} = 7.005 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 4:** Combined coefficient from energy balance.

$$h_{combined} = \frac{Q_{in}}{A_s (T_s - T_\infty)} = \frac{42}{0.05027 \times 77} = \frac{42}{3.871} = 10.85 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 5:** Radiation fraction.

$$f_{rad} = \frac{h_r}{h_{combined}} = \frac{7.005}{10.85} = 0.6456 = 64.56\%$$

**Final Answers:**
- $h_r \approx 7.0 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$
- $h_{combined} \approx 10.85 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$
- Radiation share $\approx 64.6\%$

**(b)** Explain with a labeled diagram the experimental setup used to study combined convection and radiation heat transfer from a heated cylinder. Discuss **two sources of error** in the experiment and their remedies. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Apparatus (label list):** DC power supply, ammeter, voltmeter, heater coil, thermocouples (for surface and ambient), enclosure to maintain uniform $T_{surr}$, and a viewing window.

**Procedure:** Voltage is applied in steps. Once steady state is reached at each step, surface and ambient temperatures are recorded. $V$, $I$, $T_s$, $T_\infty$, and $T_{surr}$ are tabulated.

**Source of Error 1 — Heat loss through lead wires and supports.**
*Remedy:* Use thin, low-conductivity lead wires; ensure end losses are negligible compared to side losses by choosing $L/D \geq 10$.

**Source of Error 2 — Uncertainty in surface emissivity.**
*Remedy:* Use published tables for oxidized copper ($\varepsilon \approx 0.78$); maintain surface cleanliness; or calibrate with a blackbody comparison.

**Source of Error 3 — Non-uniform enclosure temperature $T_{surr}$.**
*Remedy:* Insulate the surrounding chamber walls and measure $T_{surr}$ at multiple locations to obtain an average.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]*

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **radiation heat transfer coefficient** $h_r$ starting from Stefan–Boltzmann Law. Show clearly how it combines with $h_c$ to give a unified overall coefficient. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution Outline:**

1. Stefan–Boltzmann gives $q_{rad} = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_s^4 - T_{surr}^4)$.
2. Apply the identity $(T_s^4 - T_{surr}^4) = (T_s - T_{surr})(T_s + T_{surr})(T_s^2 + T_{surr}^2)$.
3. So $q_{rad} = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_s - T_{surr})(T_s + T_{surr})(T_s^2 + T_{surr}^2)$.
4. By definition, $h_r$ must satisfy $q_{rad} = h_r A (T_s - T_{surr})$.
5. Equating: $h_r = \varepsilon \sigma (T_s + T_{surr})(T_s^2 + T_{surr}^2)$.
6. Total heat: $q = q_{conv} + q_{rad} = (h_c + h_r) A (T_s - T_\infty)$.
7. Hence $h_{overall} = h_c + h_r$.

**(b)** A polished aluminum strip ($\varepsilon = 0.05$) is heated to $T_s = 500 \ \text{K}$ in surroundings at $T_{surr} = 320 \ \text{K}$ and ambient air at $T_\infty = 300 \ \text{K}$. The convection coefficient is $h_c = 8 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$. Compare the **fraction of heat lost by radiation** vs. **convection** for this strip vs. an oxidized copper strip ($\varepsilon = 0.78$) under identical conditions. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Radiation coefficient for polished aluminum.**

$$h_{r,Al} = 0.05 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (500 + 320)(500^2 + 320^2)$$

$$h_{r,Al} = 0.05 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 820 \times 352400$$

$$h_{r,Al} = 2.835 \times 10^{-9} \times 2.8897 \times 10^{8} = 0.819 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Combined coefficient for aluminum.**

$$h_{comb,Al} = 8 + 0.819 = 8.819 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

$$f_{rad,Al} = \frac{0.819}{8.819} = 9.29\%$$

**Step 3 — Radiation coefficient for oxidized copper.**

$$h_{r,Cu} = 0.78 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 820 \times 352400 = 12.78 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 4 — Combined coefficient for copper.**

$$h_{comb,Cu} = 8 + 12.78 = 20.78 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

$$f_{rad,Cu} = \frac{12.78}{20.78} = 61.5\%$$

**Step 5 — Comment.**
For polished aluminum, despite the high surface temperature (500 K), the radiation fraction is only **~9%** due to its low emissivity. For oxidized copper under identical conditions, radiation is **~62%** of the total heat loss. This proves that **surface emissivity**, not just temperature, controls radiation losses. Polished reflective surfaces are therefore preferred in **cryogenic insulation** and **thermal shielding** applications.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Forgetting to convert °C to K** before substituting into $T^4$ terms. Always convert first.
> - **Using cylinder surface area wrongly** as $\pi r^2$ (end cap) instead of $\pi D L$ (lateral). The lab heater loses heat from the curved surface only.
> - **Confusing $T_\infty$ with $T_{surr}$**. $T_\infty$ is the air temperature (used in convection); $T_{surr}$ is the enclosure wall temperature (used in radiation).
> - **Skipping the energy balance check**. Always verify $V \cdot I \approx h_{combined} \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$. If the numbers don't match within 5–10%, the steady state wasn't reached.
> - **Using ambient $T_\infty$ instead of $T_{surr}$ in $h_r$ formula**. This is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Two modes act in parallel**, so their coefficients **add** ($h_{overall} = h_c + h_r$), not their resistances.
- **Always use Kelvin** in all radiation formulas ($T^4$ terms).
- **Emissivity ($\varepsilon$)** is the single most influential surface property for radiation.
- **Stefan–Boltzmann constant** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ — memorize it.
- **Energy balance** is the core of the experiment: $V \cdot I = h_{combined} \cdot A_s \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)$.
- **At low temperatures (< 350 K)**, convection dominates; **above 400 K**, radiation becomes significant for high-$\varepsilon$ surfaces.
- **Difference-of-fourth-powers identity** is the bridge between $T^4$ nonlinearity and the linear $\Delta T$ form.
- **Oxidized copper ($\varepsilon \approx 0.78$)** is the standard lab heater surface — values around 0.7 are reasonable defaults.
- **Polished aluminum ($\varepsilon \approx 0.05$)** is a near-perfect thermal reflector — used in radiation shields.
- **Steady-state** is mandatory — wait until temperature readings stabilize before recording.
- **View factor** for a small object in a large room $\approx 1$, simplifying the radiation expression.
- **Lab record essentials**: tabulate $V$, $I$, $T_s$, $T_\infty$, $T_{surr}$, compute $Q_{in}$, $h_r$, $h_c$, $h_{combined}$, and radiation % at every steady-state point.
- **KTU 2024 Scheme emphasis**: The viva often asks *"Why does $h_r$ increase with temperature?"* — answer: because it contains the factor $(T_s + T_{surr})(T_s^2 + T_{surr}^2)$, which grows steeply with temperature.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radiation Heat Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL408 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Radiation Heat Transfer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Radiation Heat Transfer** is the mode of heat transfer that occurs by the emission of electromagnetic waves (or photons) from the surface of a body, without requiring an intervening medium. Unlike conduction and convection, radiation can occur through a perfect vacuum. All bodies above absolute zero temperature (0 K or −273.15 °C) emit thermal radiation.

In the context of **PCCHL408 – Heat Transfer Operations Lab (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, the radiation experiment typically aims to determine the **emissivity** of a given test surface and verify the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** of radiation, which states that the radiant heat flux emitted by a black body is proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature.

$$q = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$$

where:
- $q$ = radiant heat flux (W/m²)
- $\varepsilon$ = emissivity of the surface (dimensionless, 0 ≤ ε ≤ 1)
- $\sigma$ = **Stefan-Boltzmann constant = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²K⁴**
- $T$ = absolute temperature of the surface (K)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The lab experiment focuses on practical measurement of emissivity, comparison of black body and grey body radiation, and calculation of radiative heat exchange between concentric cylinders or parallel surfaces.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine standing near a **campfire** on a cold winter night. Even though the air between you and the fire is cold, you can *feel* intense heat on your face. This heat reaches you without heating the air — it travels as **infrared electromagnetic waves** emitted by the burning wood. This is **radiation heat transfer**.

Another analogy: The **Sun warms the Earth** across 150 million kilometers of empty space. Since space is essentially a vacuum (no conduction or convection medium possible), the only way solar energy reaches Earth is through radiation. Every hot object — your body, a cup of tea, a heated metal plate — continuously radiates energy, and hotter objects radiate *much* more (fourth power of temperature!).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction from Other Modes:**
> - **Conduction** → Requires a solid/liquid/gas medium; energy transferred by molecular vibration.
> - **Convection** → Requires a fluid medium; energy carried by bulk fluid motion.
> - **Radiation** → Requires **NO medium**; energy transferred by electromagnetic waves traveling at the speed of light ($c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s).

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Properties

| Property | Symbol | Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stefan-Boltzmann constant | $\sigma$ | **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²K⁴** | Universal radiation constant |
| Speed of light | $c$ | **3 × 10⁸ m/s** | Propagation of EM waves |
| Emissivity (black body) | $\varepsilon$ | 1 | Maximum possible emissivity |
| Emissivity (polished metal) | $\varepsilon$ | 0.02 – 0.10 | Very low emitter |
| Emissivity (human skin) | $\varepsilon$ | ≈ 0.95 | Near-black body |
| Wavelength range for thermal radiation | $\lambda$ | 0.1 – 100 μm | Infrared + visible spectrum |

> [!TIP]
> **Remember:** A perfect emitter ($\varepsilon = 1$) is called a **black body**. A surface with $\varepsilon < 1$ that emits uniformly across all wavelengths is called a **grey body**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Radiation Concepts

The physics of thermal radiation rests on three fundamental phenomena that occur when radiation strikes a surface:

- **Absorption (α):** The fraction of incident radiation absorbed by the surface.
- **Reflection (ρ):** The fraction of incident radiation reflected by the surface.
- **Transmission (τ):** The fraction of incident radiation transmitted through the surface.

These three fractions must sum to unity (energy conservation):

$$\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$$

For **opaque bodies** (most engineering surfaces, e.g., metals), $\tau = 0$, so:

$$\alpha + \rho = 1$$

## 2.2 Black Body Radiation

A **black body** is an idealized surface that:
1. Absorbs **all** incident radiation ($\alpha = 1$, $\rho = 0$, $\tau = 0$).
2. Emits the **maximum possible** radiation at any given temperature.
3. Is a **diffuse emitter** (radiates uniformly in all directions).

The total emissive power of a black body is given by the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**:

$$E_b = \sigma T^{4} \quad \text{[W/m}^{2}\text{]}$$

The spectral distribution of black body radiation follows **Planck's Law**:

$$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{2\pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{hc}{\lambda k_B T}\right) - 1 \right]^{-1}$$

where $h$ = Planck's constant = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s, and $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K.

## 2.3 Grey Body & Emissivity

A **grey body** has an emissivity $\varepsilon < 1$ that is constant across all wavelengths. Its emissive power is:

$$E = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$$

## 2.4 Kirchhoff's Law

**Kirchhoff's Law** of thermal radiation establishes the equivalence between emissivity and absorptivity for a surface in thermal equilibrium:

$$\varepsilon = \alpha$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Meaning:** A good absorber is also a good emitter. Polished silver has low absorptivity (≈0.02), so it is also a poor emitter. A black-painted surface has high absorptivity (≈0.97), so it is also an excellent emitter. This is why **refrigerator interiors are white/polished** (low ε, slow heat gain) and **radiator fins are black-painted** (high ε, rapid heat loss).

## 2.5 View Factor (Geometric Factor / Shape Factor)

When two surfaces exchange radiation, the fraction of radiation leaving surface 1 that strikes surface 2 is called the **view factor** $F_{1 \to 2}$ (or $F_{12}$).

Key view factor relations:
- **Reciprocity:** $A_1 F_{1 \to 2} = A_2 F_{2 \to 1}$
- **Summation rule:** $\sum_{j=1}^{N} F_{i \to j} = 1$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Quick Reference)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_b = \sigma T^{4}$ | Stefan-Boltzmann (black body emissive power) | W/m² |
| 2 | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$ | Grey body emissive power | W/m² |
| 3 | $Q = \varepsilon \sigma A T^{4}$ | Total radiation emitted by a surface | W |
| 4 | $Q_{1 \to 2} = A_1 F_{1 \to 2} \sigma \left(T_1^{4} - T_2^{4}\right)$ | Net heat exchange between two black bodies | W |
| 5 | $q_{net} = \varepsilon \sigma \left(T_1^{4} - T_2^{4}\right)$ | Net flux between two infinite parallel plates | W/m² |
| 6 | $\varepsilon = \alpha$ | Kirchhoff's law (at thermal equilibrium) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$ | Energy balance on incident radiation | dimensionless |
| 8 | $A_1 F_{1 \to 2} = A_2 F_{2 \to 1}$ | Reciprocity relation | m² |
| 9 | $\lambda_{max} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ | Wien's displacement law | m·K |
| 10 | $\lambda_m = \frac{2898}{T}$ | Peak wavelength of emission | μm |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting the **4th power of temperature**! Radiation is *extremely sensitive* to temperature. Doubling absolute temperature increases radiation by a factor of $2^4 = 16$ times.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Furnaces & Boilers:** Combustion chambers reach 1000–1800 K; radiation is the dominant heat transfer mode.
- **Solar Collectors:** Absorber coatings are designed with high $\alpha$ for solar spectrum and low $\varepsilon$ for IR (selective surfaces).
- **Re-entry Vehicles & Spacecraft:** Thermal protection tiles (high ε) radiate heat to deep space.
- **Building Insulation:** Radiant barriers in attics reflect IR radiation, reducing cooling loads.
- **Infrared Thermography:** Medical diagnostics, military target detection, building audits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Net Radiative Heat Exchange Between Two Concentric Cylinders (Lab Apparatus Configuration)

This is the **standard geometry** used in the KTU radiation lab experiment: a small heated cylinder (test surface) placed concentrically inside a larger cooled cylinder (receiver).

**Given:**
- Inner cylinder (1): radius $r_1$, surface area $A_1 = 2\pi r_1 L$, absolute temperature $T_1$
- Outer cylinder (2): radius $r_2$, surface area $A_2 = 2\pi r_2 L$, absolute temperature $T_2$
- Emissivities: $\varepsilon_1$ (inner), $\varepsilon_2$ (outer)
- Length: $L \gg r_2$ (so end effects are neglected)

**Step 1 — Net radiation exchange for black bodies:**

For two black bodies forming an enclosure where surface 1 "sees" only surface 2 (concentric geometry), the view factor $F_{1 \to 2} = 1$.

$$Q_{1 \to 2}^{\text{black}} = A_1 \sigma \left(T_1^{4} - T_2^{4}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Accounting for real (grey) surfaces:**

When both surfaces have emissivities $\varepsilon_1 < 1$ and $\varepsilon_2 < 1$, we must account for **multiple reflections** between the surfaces. Each surface absorbs only a fraction of incident radiation, and reflects the rest back to the other surface. The effective emissivity of the system is:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} = \left[ \frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{A_1}{A_2}\left(\frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1\right) \right]^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Justification of the effective emissivity:**

Consider a ray of radiation leaving surface 1:
- Surface 2 absorbs a fraction $\varepsilon_2$ of it.
- Surface 2 reflects a fraction $(1 - \varepsilon_2)$ back to surface 1.
- Surface 1 absorbs a fraction $\varepsilon_1$ of this reflected ray.
- This bouncing continues infinitely.

Summing the infinite geometric series of absorbed radiation gives the effective emissivity formula above. For the **lab derivation** (concentric cylinders), $A_1 < A_2$, so the term $\frac{A_1}{A_2}\left(\frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1\right)$ is small, and the dominant resistance to radiation comes from the inner surface.

**Step 4 — Final net heat transfer equation:**

$$Q_{1 \to 2} = \varepsilon_{\text{eff}} \, A_1 \, \sigma \left(T_1^{4} - T_2^{4}\right)$$

Substituting the effective emissivity:

$$Q_{1 \to 2} = \frac{\sigma A_1 \left(T_1^{4} - T_2^{4}\right)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1\right)}$$

This is the **key working equation** used to analyze the KTU radiation lab experiment.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Lab Style)

**Problem:** A cylindrical heater of diameter 50 mm and length 200 mm is placed coaxially inside a polished metal tube of diameter 150 mm. The heater surface is at $T_1 = 800$ K with emissivity $\varepsilon_1 = 0.85$. The outer tube is at $T_2 = 350$ K with emissivity $\varepsilon_2 = 0.10$. Calculate the net radiative heat transfer.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute surface areas:**

$$A_1 = \pi d_1 L = \pi \times 0.050 \times 0.200 = 0.0314 \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$A_2 = \pi d_2 L = \pi \times 0.150 \times 0.200 = 0.0942 \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute temperature difference term:**

$$T_1^{4} - T_2^{4} = (800)^{4} - (350)^{4}$$

Computing each term:

$$(800)^{4} = 800 \times 800 \times 800 \times 800 = 4.096 \times 10^{11} \text{ K}^{4}$$

$$(350)^{4} = 350 \times 350 \times 350 \times 350 = 1.5006 \times 10^{10} \text{ K}^{4}$$

$$T_1^{4} - T_2^{4} = 4.096 \times 10^{11} - 1.5006 \times 10^{10} = 3.946 \times 10^{11} \text{ K}^{4}$$

**Step 3 — Compute effective emissivity:**

$$\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} = \left[ \frac{1}{0.85} + \frac{0.0314}{0.0942}\left(\frac{1}{0.10} - 1\right) \right]^{-1}$$

Compute the bracket term:

$$\frac{1}{0.85} = 1.1765$$

$$\frac{0.0314}{0.0942} = 0.3333$$

$$\frac{1}{0.10} - 1 = 10 - 1 = 9$$

$$\frac{A_1}{A_2}\left(\frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1\right) = 0.3333 \times 9 = 3.000$$

$$1.1765 + 3.000 = 4.1765$$

$$\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{4.1765} = 0.2394$$

**Step 4 — Compute net radiative heat transfer:**

$$Q_{1 \to 2} = 0.2394 \times 0.0314 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 3.946 \times 10^{11}$$

Multiplying step by step:

$$0.2394 \times 0.0314 = 7.518 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$7.518 \times 10^{-3} \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} = 4.263 \times 10^{-10}$$

$$4.263 \times 10^{-10} \times 3.946 \times 10^{11} = 168.2 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{1 \to 2} \approx 168.2 \text{ W}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** The polished outer tube ($\varepsilon_2 = 0.10$) drastically reduces the net heat transfer (from ≈ 700 W if both were black bodies down to 168 W). This demonstrates **Kirchhoff's Law** in practice — highly reflective surfaces are also poor absorbers, blocking heat transfer.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Radiation Lab Apparatus

This diagram maps the **functional flow** of components, signal paths, and control loops in a typical KTU radiation heat transfer experimental setup.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Mains Supply 230V 50Hz] --> B[Variac Autotransformer]
    B --> C[Heater Element NiCr Coil]
    C --> D[Test Specimen Cylinder]
    D --> E[Outer Receiver Cylinder Polished]
    D --> F[Inner Surface Temperature T1]
    E --> G[Outer Surface Temperature T2]
    F --> H[Multichannel Digital Indicator]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Data Logging to PC]
    H --> J[Display Temperature in C]
    E --> K[Cooling Water Inlet]
    E --> L[Cooling Water Outlet]
    E --> M[Flow Meter Rotameter]
    K --> M
    M --> E
    H --> N[Emissivity Calculation Module]
    N --> O[Comparison with Stefan Boltzmann Law]
    O --> P[Result Heat Transfer Rate in W]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#1e40af,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#0891b2,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    style P fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Radiation Heat Transfer Mechanism Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Hot Surface T1] -- emits EM waves --> M1[Vacuum or Air Gap]
    M1 -- arrives at --> P2[Cold Surface T2]
    P2 -- absorbs fraction alpha --> A1[Absorbed Energy Qabs]
    P2 -- reflects fraction rho --> R1[Reflected Energy Qref]
    P2 -- transmits fraction tau --> T1[Transmitted Energy Qtrans]
    P2 -- re-emits thermal radiation --> M1

    style P1 fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    style P2 fill:#0369a1,color:#ffffff
    style M1 fill:#facc15,color:#000000
    style A1 fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    style R1 fill:#f97316,color:#ffffff
    style T1 fill:#a855f7,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Kirchhoff's Law — Emitter/Absorber Equivalence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Surface Classification]
    S1 --> S2[Black Body]
    S1 --> S3[Grey Body]
    S1 --> S4[Real Selective Surface]
    S2 --> P1[alpha equals 1]
    S2 --> P2[epsilon equals 1]
    S2 --> P3[rho equals 0]
    S3 --> P4[alpha equals epsilon less than 1]
    S3 --> P5[Constant across wavelengths]
    S4 --> P6[alpha high in solar spectrum]
    S4 --> P7[epsilon low in IR spectrum]
    P4 --> K1[Kirchhoff's Law alpha equals epsilon]
    P6 --> K1
    P7 --> K1

    style S1 fill:#1e293b,color:#ffffff
    style S2 fill:#000000,color:#ffffff
    style S3 fill:#6b7280,color:#ffffff
    style S4 fill:#0d9488,color:#ffffff
    style K1 fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.4 Experimental Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StepA[Step 1 Polish Test Cylinder] --> StepB[Step 2 Insert in Receiver]
    StepB --> StepC[Step 3 Connect Thermocouples]
    StepC --> StepD[Step 4 Set Variac to Low Voltage]
    StepD --> StepE[Step 5 Switch ON Heater]
    StepE --> StepF[Step 6 Wait for Steady State 30 min]
    StepF --> StepG[Step 7 Record T1 T2 V I]
    StepG --> StepH[Step 8 Repeat for Higher Voltages]
    StepH --> StepI[Step 9 Plot T1 to the power 4 minus T2 to the power 4 vs Heat Input]
    StepI --> StepJ[Step 10 Calculate Emissivity from Slope]
    StepJ --> StepK[Step 11 Compare with Theoretical Value]

    style StepA fill:#0369a1,color:#ffffff
    style StepE fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    style StepF fill:#ca8a04,color:#ffffff
    style StepJ fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    style StepK fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State and explain Stefan-Boltzmann Law. Define the Stefan-Boltzmann constant with its SI units.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** states that the total radiant heat energy emitted per unit time per unit area of a black body is directly proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature.

$$E_b = \sigma T^{4}$$

The constant of proportionality $\sigma$ is the **Stefan-Boltzmann constant** with value $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²K⁴. It represents the radiant energy emitted per second per square meter of a black body at 1 K.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the following terms: (i) Emissivity, (ii) Absorptivity, (iii) Black body.**

**Model Answer:**
- **(i) Emissivity ($\varepsilon$):** The ratio of radiation emitted by a real surface to the radiation emitted by a black body at the same temperature. Range: 0 ≤ $\varepsilon$ ≤ 1.
- **(ii) Absorptivity ($\alpha$):** The fraction of incident radiation absorbed by a surface, ranging from 0 to 1.
- **(iii) Black body:** An idealized surface that absorbs all incident radiation ($\alpha = 1$) and emits maximum possible radiation at any given temperature. It is a perfect emitter and a perfect absorber.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**
**(a)** Derive the expression for the net radiative heat exchange between two concentric cylinders of radii $r_1$ and $r_2$ (with $r_1 < r_2$), emissivities $\varepsilon_1$ and $\varepsilon_2$, and temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$ respectively. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A small metal sphere of diameter 20 mm at 1200 K is suspended in a large evacuated enclosure whose walls are at 400 K. If the sphere's emissivity is 0.6, calculate: (i) the net rate of heat loss by the sphere, and (ii) the wavelength at which maximum emission occurs. **[7 marks]**

### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Derivation:**

**Step 1:** For two black bodies forming a concentric enclosure, the view factor $F_{1 \to 2} = 1$ (all radiation leaving surface 1 strikes surface 2).

$$Q_{\text{black}} = A_1 \sigma \left(T_1^{4} - T_2^{4}\right)$$

**Step 2:** For grey bodies, account for multiple reflections. Let $J_1$ and $J_2$ be the radiosities. By energy balance:

$$J_1 = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^{4} + (1 - \varepsilon_1) J_2$$

$$J_2 = \varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^{4} + (1 - \varepsilon_2) J_1$$

**Step 3:** Solving simultaneously and substituting $Q = \frac{J_1 - J_2}{\frac{1 - \varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} + \frac{1}{A_1 F_{1 \to 2}} + \frac{1 - \varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2}}$:

With $F_{1 \to 2} = 1$:

$$Q = \frac{\sigma \left(T_1^{4} - T_2^{4}\right)}{\dfrac{1 - \varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} + \dfrac{1}{A_1} + \dfrac{1 - \varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2}}$$

Rearranging:

$$Q = \frac{\sigma A_1 \left(T_1^{4} - T_2^{4}\right)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1\right)}$$

This is the standard two-surface enclosure formula. **(+7 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $d = 20$ mm = 0.020 m, $T_1 = 1200$ K, $T_2 = 400$ K, $\varepsilon_1 = 0.6$.

Since the enclosure is **large** ($A_2 \gg A_1$), $\frac{A_1}{A_2} \to 0$, so $\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} \to \varepsilon_1$.

**(i) Net heat loss:**

$$A_1 = \pi d^{2} = \pi (0.020)^{2} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$T_1^{4} - T_2^{4} = (1200)^{4} - (400)^{4} = 2.0736 \times 10^{12} - 2.56 \times 10^{10} = 2.048 \times 10^{12} \text{ K}^{4}$$

$$Q = 0.6 \times 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.048 \times 10^{12}$$

$$Q = 0.6 \times 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.1612 \times 10^{5} = 87.55 \text{ W}$$

**(ii) Peak wavelength** (Wien's law):

$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{2898}{T_1} = \frac{2898}{1200} = 2.415 \text{ μm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 4th power:** Writing $Q = hA\Delta T$ instead of $Q \propto (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$. This is a **3-mark** error!
> 2. **Unit mismatch:** Mixing °C and K. **All radiation calculations MUST use Kelvin.**
> 3. **Ignoring effective emissivity:** Assuming $\varepsilon = 1$ for non-black bodies. The KTU answer key strictly penalizes this.
> 4. **Skipping the "large enclosure" approximation:** Forgetting that $A_2/A_1 \to \infty$ makes $\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} = \varepsilon_1$.

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**
**(a)** State and explain **Kirchhoff's Law** of thermal radiation. How is it experimentally verified? **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Two large parallel plates are maintained at temperatures 1000 K and 500 K respectively. The emissivities of the two surfaces are 0.6 and 0.3. Calculate the net heat transfer per unit area between the plates. **[7 marks]**

### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Kirchhoff's Law:**

**Statement:** At thermal equilibrium, the emissivity of a body equals its absorptivity for radiation of a given wavelength and temperature.

$$\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$$

**Explanation:** A good absorber is also a good emitter. The law arises from the principle of detailed balance — if a body absorbed more than it emitted at some wavelength, it would heat up indefinitely, violating the second law of thermodynamics.

**Experimental Verification:** A body (e.g., a blackened metal plate) is placed in an evacuated enclosure. The walls of the enclosure are heated uniformly. At steady state, the test body reaches the same temperature as the walls. The rate of emission from the body must equal the rate of absorption, which implies $\varepsilon = \alpha$. Polished silver ($\varepsilon = 0.02$, $\alpha = 0.02$) and black paint ($\varepsilon = 0.95$, $\alpha = 0.95$) confirm this in practice. **(+7 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $T_1 = 1000$ K, $T_2 = 500$ K, $\varepsilon_1 = 0.6$, $\varepsilon_2 = 0.3$, $A_1 = A_2 = A$ (parallel plates, so $\frac{A_1}{A_2} = 1$).

Effective emissivity:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} = \left[ \frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1 \right]^{-1} = \left[ \frac{1}{0.6} + \frac{1}{0.3} - 1 \right]^{-1}$$

$$\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} = \left[ 1.6667 + 3.3333 - 1 \right]^{-1} = \left[ 4.000 \right]^{-1} = 0.25$$

Temperature term:

$$T_1^{4} - T_2^{4} = (1000)^{4} - (500)^{4} = 10^{12} - 6.25 \times 10^{10} = 9.375 \times 10^{11} \text{ K}^{4}$$

Net heat flux:
