## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Question Paper Style]**

**Q1. Define the term *Fin Efficiency* and state the mathematical expression for a straight rectangular fin with an insulated tip.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Fin efficiency (η_fin) is the ratio of the actual heat dissipated by the fin to the heat that would be dissipated if the entire fin were at the base temperature.

$$
\eta_{fin} = \dfrac{Q_{fin}}{Q_{ideal}} = \dfrac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}
$$

where $m = \sqrt{\dfrac{hP}{kA_c}}$ and L is the length of the fin.

---

**Q2. Distinguish between *Fin Efficiency* and *Fin Effectiveness*.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Definition | Value Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Fin Efficiency** (η_fin) | Ratio of actual heat transfer to the heat transfer if the entire fin were at base temperature. | 0 < η_fin < 1 |
| **Fin Effectiveness** (ε_fin) | Ratio of heat transfer with fin to heat transfer without fin (i.e., with bare surface). | ε_fin > 1 (otherwise fin is useless) |

Mathematically:

$$
\varepsilon_{fin} = \eta_{fin}\cdot\dfrac{A_f}{A_c}
$$

A fin is justified in engineering design only if ε_fin ≥ 2 (commonly accepted KTU rule of thumb).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Model)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Following the KTU 2024 Scheme pattern, two alternative full-mark questions are provided. Students answer ONE of them.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [Commonly asked — focuses on derivation + numerical]

**(a)** Derive the general differential equation governing steady-state 1-D heat conduction in a uniform pin fin with convection from its lateral surface. Clearly state all assumptions.
*(7 Marks)*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Define the element:** Consider a differential element of the fin of thickness dx at a distance x from the base.
2. **State assumptions:**
   - Steady-state ($\partial T/\partial t = 0$)
   - 1-D conduction
   - Constant k, h, A_c, P
   - Constant ambient temperature T_∞
   - No internal heat generation
   - Negligible radiation
3. **Energy balance:**
   $Q_x = Q_{x+dx} + Q_c$
4. **Substitute expressions** and simplify:

$$
kA_c \dfrac{d^2 T}{dx^2} + hP\,(T-T_\infty) = 0
$$

5. **Introduce excess temperature** θ = T − T_∞ and parameter $m^2 = \dfrac{hP}{kA_c}$:

$$
\dfrac{d^2\theta}{dx^2} - m^2\theta = 0
$$

6. **General solution:** $\theta(x) = C_1 e^{mx} + C_2 e^{-mx}$

7. **Apply insulated-tip BC** → final distribution

$$
\theta(x) = \theta_b\,\dfrac{\cosh\!\big(m(L-x)\big)}{\cosh(mL)}
$$

**(b)** A brass pin fin (k = 109 W/m·K, d = 12.5 mm, L = 100 mm) is exposed to ambient air at 30 °C. The base is maintained at 120 °C and the tip at 70 °C. Calculate (i) experimental fin efficiency, (ii) heat dissipated per fin if h = 30 W/m²·K.
*(7 Marks)*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(i) Experimental efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{exp} = \dfrac{\theta_b - \theta_L}{\theta_b} = \dfrac{(120-30) - (70-30)}{120-30} = \dfrac{90 - 40}{90} = \dfrac{50}{90} = 0.556
$$

**(ii) Cross-sectional area:**

$$
A_c = \dfrac{\pi (0.0125)^2}{4} = 1.227 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^2
$$

**Perimeter:**

$$
P = \pi(0.0125) = 0.0393\,\text{m}
$$

**Fin parameter m:**

$$
m = \sqrt{\dfrac{hP}{kA_c}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{30 \times 0.0393}{109 \times 1.227 \times 10^{-4}}} = 9.39\,\text{m}^{-1}
$$

**mL:**

$$
mL = 9.39 \times 0.10 = 0.939
$$

**Heat dissipated:**

$$
Q = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b\,\tanh(mL)
$$

Intermediate:

$$
\sqrt{hPkA_c} = \sqrt{30 \times 0.0393 \times 109 \times 1.227 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.126\,\text{W/K}
$$

And $\tanh(0.939) = 0.736$

Therefore:

$$
Q = 0.126 \times 90 \times 0.736 = 8.35\,\text{W}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B (Numerical):**
> Students often forget to convert temperatures to **excess** values (θ = T − T_∞) before substituting. They also confuse `tanh(mL)` with `tanh⁻¹(mL)`. Both errors cost 1–2 marks per instance. Always write θ explicitly, and always double-check the function on the calculator.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative choice — focuses on apparatus + interpretation]

**(a)** With the help of a neat labelled diagram, describe the experimental setup for determining the temperature distribution along a pin-fin and explain the procedure to evaluate fin efficiency.
*(7 Marks)*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Apparatus description:**
   - A vertical brass rod (pin fin) of length ≈ 100 mm and diameter ≈ 12.5 mm.
   - An electric heater wound at the base of the rod (nichrome wire).
   - 8 thermocouples (T₁ at base … T₈ at tip) inserted in drilled holes along the fin.
   - A variac to control the heater input.
   - Digital temperature indicator.
   - Voltmeter + Ammeter to measure heater wattage.

2. **Procedure:**
   - Step 1: Assemble the apparatus; ensure all thermocouples are calibrated.
   - Step 2: Set the variac to a low voltage; switch on the heater.
   - Step 3: Allow ≈ 30 min for steady state (when T_i readings stabilize).
   - Step 4: Note ambient temperature T_∞, all fin temperatures T₁–T₈, and heater voltage/current.
   - Step 5: Compute m, mL, η_theory, η_experimental.
   - Step 6: Plot T vs x; verify exponential decay.
   - Step 7: Repeat for 2–3 different heater wattages.

**(b)** Discuss the major sources of deviation between experimental and theoretical fin efficiencies. How can these be minimised?
*(7 Marks)*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

| Source of Error | Magnitude | Minimisation Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| Neglect of radiation | High at T_b > 150 °C | Use corrected h including radiation (linearised) |
| Constant h assumption | Moderate | Use Nusselt correlations for free convection (Churchill–Chu) |
| Heat loss through fin support / heater housing | Moderate | Insulate the support with ceramic wool |
| Thermocouple contact resistance | Low–moderate | Use thermocouple paste or silver-solder the junction |
| Variable ambient temperature | Low | Conduct experiment in a closed, AC-controlled lab |
| Surface oxidation of fin (changing ε) | Long-term | Polish fin before each run |

**Concluding statement:** The deviations are primarily systematic (radiation, support loss) and can be reduced below 5 % with proper insulation and inclusion of a linearised radiation term in h_total = h_conv + h_rad.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:**
> Many students draw a free-hand sketch without proper labels. KTU valuation keys demand a **labelled block diagram with directional heat-flow arrows**. Always include (a) heater, (b) fin, (c) thermocouple locations, (d) ambient, and (e) data path. Missing labels routinely cost 2–3 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Pin-Fin experiment** simultaneously demonstrates **Conduction** (within metal) + **Convection** (from surface). It does **not** involve a phase change.
- ⭐ **Fin parameter:** $m = \sqrt{\dfrac{hP}{kA_c}}$
- ⭐ **Excess temperature:** $\theta = T - T_\infty$ (always a positive quantity)
- ⭐ **Fin differential equation:** $\dfrac{d^2\theta}{dx^2} - m^2\theta = 0$
- ⭐ **Insulated-tip solution:** $\theta(x) = \theta_b\,\dfrac{\cosh\!\big(m(L-x)\big)}{\cosh(mL)}$
- ⭐ **Heat dissipated (long fin):** $Q = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\,\theta_b\,\tanh(mL)$
- ⭐ **Fin efficiency:** $\eta_{fin} = \dfrac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}$
- ⭐ **Experimental efficiency:** $\eta_{exp} = \dfrac{\theta_b - \theta_L}{\theta_b}$
- ⭐ **Fin effectiveness** must be **> 2** for engineering justification.
- ⭐ **Boundary condition choice:** Insulated tip (default in KTU) vs. convective tip vs. prescribed tip temperature.
- ⭐ **Acceptable deviation** between η_exp and η_th in KTU labs: **5–30 %**.
- ⭐ **Steady state** must be confirmed before recording data (≈ 30 min wait).
- ⭐ **Plot T vs x** is a hyperbolic-cosine decay curve.
- ⭐ **Sources of error:** radiation, support heat loss, thermocouple contact, h variation, drafts.
- ⭐ **Real-world examples:** CPU heat sinks, motorcycle fins, transformer radiators, refrigeration coils.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** During viva voce, expect questions like *"Why does the temperature drop more sharply near the tip than at the base?"* — Answer: *"Because near the base, conduction delivers heat faster than convection can remove it, so temperature is high. Near the tip, conduction supply is low while convection still removes heat, so temperature drops sharply, approaching T_∞ asymptotically."*
